The authors introduce 3D-Fit, a token-efficient evaluation strategy, and find that LLMs still trail specialized diffusion models but can satisfy multiple spatial constraints at once.

title: GigaPath-Flash and GigaTIME-Flash: Efficient Pathology Foundation Models for Whole-Slide and Tumor Microenvironment Analysis
blurb: This paper introduces GigaPath-Flash and GigaTIME-Flash, Apache-2.0 pathology foundation models that pair a 22M-parameter ViT-S tile encoder distilled from the billion-parameter GigaPath teacher with a 21M-parameter LongNet slide encoder. GigaPath-Flash retains 97% of GigaPath's average slide-level performance with 50x less compute, and GigaTIME-Flash predicts the tumor immune microenvironment from H&E images while running 6x faster on 8x less GPU memory.

title: Can We Break LLMs Out of Self-Loops? Fine-Grained Reasoning Control with Activation Steering
blurb: This paper introduces SOPHIA, a method that treats each reasoning trace as a sequence of latent states and steers models out of self-loops at inference time. The authors classify every prefix to a latent state, build a bank of steering vectors indexed by state pairs, and use a controller that detects looping online and applies the matching vector, improving both end-task accuracy and token efficiency.

title: Generalised Bellman recurrence and three dualities in sequential decision-making
blurb: This paper derives the Bellman equation from three conditions: that dynamics decompose through sufficient statistics, that returns decompose recursively, and that uncertainty aggregation is compatible with both. The authors show the same conditions produce three dualities between probability, return, and aggregation, unifying methods developed separately across reinforcement learning, control, and decision theory, and suggest state augmentation when a condition fails.

title: FinSAgent: Corpus-Aligned Multi-Agent RAG Framework for Evidence-Grounded SEC Filing Question Answering
blurb: FinSAgent is a multi-agent RAG framework for question answering over SEC filings that conditions retrieval on the corpus itself rather than on model priors. It assigns agents to the mandated 10-K item structure, decomposes queries against a summary-level view of the local filings, and uses a feature-gated reranker that separates evidential validity from semantic similarity, improving retrieval coverage and answer correctness across five benchmarks.

title: Vector Search As Nearest Neighbor Matching: RAG-based Policy Learning in Causal Inference
blurb: This paper formulates retrieval-augmented generation as a policy learning problem, showing that action-specific vector search is equivalent to nearest-neighbor matching in causal inference. The authors propose one-step and two-step methods under the potential outcome framework, and decompose the two-step method's regret into candidate-generation and within-candidate choice components, bounding the latter with prediction-error guarantees for nearest-neighbor estimators and transformers.

title: AdaHome: An Adaptive Smart Home Assistant using Local Small Language Models
blurb: AdaHome is a smart home assistant built for small language models running locally, using an intent-aware planner that routes simple device commands to prompt-based handling and ambiguous ones to a lightweight Chain-of-Draft reasoning path. It hits 86.7% accuracy on direct commands with up to 3x lower latency, and its feedback-based preference adaptation reaches 88% consistency across multi-turn sessions versus 52.5% for prompt augmentation.

title: MADA-RL: Multi-Agent Debate-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Parameter-Efficient Reasoning in Compact Models
blurb: MADA-RL is a post-training framework that specializes compact models under 4B parameters into generator and critic roles, training only LoRA adapters with a debate-aware reward signal. The counterfactual critic advantage scores a critic against the generator ensemble's per-instance accuracy rather than a static baseline, lifting DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B from 39.9% to 41.9% across five math benchmarks with 16 times fewer trainable parameters than full fine-tuning.

title: What Transfers Under Source Shift? Definitions, Examples, and Fine-Tuning for Climate Disclosure Classification
blurb: This paper tests whether definitions, few-shot examples, and fine-tuning still help when climate disclosure classifiers move between annual reports, press releases, and earnings calls, evaluating eleven open- and closed-source LLMs across two corpora with a shared label space. Similarity-based retrieval and LoRA fine-tuning win in-source but lose most of that edge under source shift, while randomly selected few-shot examples and granularity-matched definitions transfer more reliably.

title: AlphaOracle: Oracle bone script decipherment via human-workflow-inspired deep learning
blurb: AlphaOracle is a decipherment framework for oracle bone script that mirrors expert philological workflow across four stages: rubbing parsing, radical-based morphological analysis, contextual retrieval, and validation against classical sources. Each stage emits confidence-weighted evidence chains, and in a study of 86 specialists the system cut analysis time by 64% while resolving the character "Lao" as a toponymic or clan designation.

title: DeLIVeR: Decomposed Learning for Information-grounded Veracity Recognition via Reinforced Knowledge Graph Exploration
blurb: DeLIVeR is a fact-checking framework that uses a Planner LLM to decompose claims into targeted question sets, then traverses knowledge graphs for evidence rather than relying on brittle retrieval queries. The authors train the planner with Group Relative Policy Optimization, rewarding structural diversity and verdict accuracy, and report F1 scores of 83.73 on LIAR, 84.57 on FEVER, and 79.70 on PolitiFact with Qwen2.5-7B, 10-15% above HippoRAG2.

title: When a Name Is Not a Name: A Benchmark Dataset and Distilled Reasoning for Culturally Entangled Bangla Homographs in Low-Resource LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces Culturally Entangled Homograph disambiguation and a Bangla benchmark of 1,516 expert-verified sentences with 3,032 labelled occurrences, where each word carries both a personal-name and a common-noun reading. Models across open and closed source default to the common-noun sense, and distilling cultural explanations into 1-3B models cut that dominant-meaning bias from as high as 100% to under 5%.

title: ESCUCHA: A Spanish Speech Benchmark for Heterogeneous Acoustic Conditions
blurb: ESCUCHA is a Spanish speech understanding benchmark for large audio language models, built from 1,000 human-curated questions paired with 162.9 hours of audio sourced from the wild rather than existing datasets. It spans 9 perceptual and 10 reasoning categories across multiple Spanish accents and non-normative speech, with clips ranging from seconds to over 80 minutes. Current state-of-the-art multimodal and speech models trail trained humans by a wide margin.

title: When to Use Extra Context: Evidence-Grounded Terminology Adaptation for Simultaneous Speech Translation
blurb: This paper introduces EGTA, a framework for simultaneous speech translation that builds a document terminology memory and injects only the terms relevant to the current streaming segment rather than the full document context. On the MCIF-dev technical-talk benchmark, EGTA improved BLEU by 1.05 on En→Zh and lifted named-entity recall by 79% relative, with shuffled-memory controls indicating the gains come from paper-specific term alignment rather than generic context prompting.

title: KernelDiag: Agent-Based Root Cause Diagnosis for Kernel Crashes
blurb: KernelDiag is an agent-based framework for diagnosing Linux kernel crashes, aligning heterogeneous artifacts like syscalls, logs, and crash reports through log-to-code mapping before specialized agents reason over source-level semantics. On the KGYM benchmark, it builds structured Evidence Graphs for faulty-method localization and beats existing approaches on Top@k accuracy, with up to 4x gains at the file level in settings without explicit hints.

title: DepRepair: LLM-Based Source-Code Repair for Dependency Breaking Changes
blurb: DepRepair is a single-call LLM approach that repairs consumer code broken by dependency updates, using an evidence filter, a usage locator, and a subcategory-aware repair guide to ground fixes in upstream release notes and API diffs. On DepBench, a new benchmark of 95 real-world dependency updates across four ecosystems with Docker-based test oracles, it reached an 89.5% pass rate with GPT-5.5 and 82.1% with Claude Opus 4.6.

title: Grok's CLI caught uploading all your local files to the cloud
blurb: Developers found that xAI's Grok CLI uploaded local files to the cloud without disclosure, surfacing just as the tool gained traction on the back of the Grok 4.5 coding model. The same issue covers engineering leaders reporting rising code review load from AI-generated pull requests and enterprise developers pushing back on higher-than-expected pricing tiers.

title: Reasoning as a Double-Edged Sword: Architecture and Cross-Stage Robustness in Vision-Language-Action Models
blurb: This paper tests whether adding a reasoning step makes Vision-Language-Action models more robust, perturbing three architectures at the vision, reasoning, and action stages on LIBERO and SimplerEnv. The latent-iterative model collapses under both stochastic noise and white-box attack while the no-reasoning and chain-of-thought models hold, and runtime safety monitors that look near-perfect under naive evaluation fall to chance under adaptive attack.

title: D-NOVA: In-Storage Retrieval Accelerator via Dual-Bound 3D NAND-Optimized Similarity Search with Vector Adaptation
blurb: D-NOVA is an in-storage retrieval accelerator that embeds RAG vector search directly into the NAND memory array rather than running it on a host or embedded processor. It pairs a NAND-friendly distance metric, Dual-Bound Tight Similarity Sensing, with a contrastive adapter that maps embeddings into that domain, reaching 12.13x higher throughput than existing in-storage RAG accelerators and up to 41.7x faster retrieval than a CPU baseline.

title: The Autonomous Agency Scale: A Behavioral Framework for Measuring Self-Directed Behavior in AI Systems
blurb: This paper introduces the Autonomous Agency Scale, a framework that scores AI systems 0-5 across seven dimensions of agency, including temporal persistence, goal formation, and self-awareness. Each dimension is scored in two bands: Active, covering user-initiated work, and Ambient, covering idle periods. Applied to six systems, task agents like Claude Code and Manus scored 2.3-2.4 Active but only 0.6-1.9 Ambient, with all idle behavior traceable to user-configured schedules.

title: TaskArtisan: Designing Composable Generative Widgets for LLM-Assisted Analysis
blurb: TaskArtisan is a research probe that lets people build and assemble generative UI widgets for analysis tasks, supporting both sequential and fan-out composition instead of a single long chat thread. In a 12-person comparison study against a chatbot, the generated GUIs improved clarity and visual presentation but introduced rigidity and new prompting difficulties, which the authors distill into a design framework covering malleability, specification, and interoperability.

title: Substack partners with AI-detection tool Pangram, allowing users to scan text longer than 100 words for an estimate of how much was written with AI assistance (Chris Best/The Substack Post)
blurb: Substack partnered with AI-detection tool Pangram to let users scan posts longer than 100 words for an estimate of how much text was written with AI assistance. CEO Chris Best framed the feature as a disclosure tool rather than an enforcement mechanism, leaving writers to decide how to present the results to readers.

title: A Practical Guide to Reducing Token Spend
blurb: This post argues that embedding complex logic in agent skills is inefficient, and that restructuring the same code review workload as a "swamp workflow" cut token usage 8x and halved runtime. The original Garfield skill burned roughly 4.5M tokens across 23 sub-agents over 12 minutes, with a coordinator agent dispatching rolling review cycles and adjudicating findings.

title: China AI Companies Rush to Raise Funds and Close Gap With U.S.
blurb: Chinese AI companies are racing to raise capital as researchers and executives point to shortages of investment and advanced chips as the main constraints on catching up with American labs. Export controls have left domestic firms competing for limited compute while U.S. rivals close multibillion-dollar infrastructure deals.

title: Sparse Evidence Can Suffice: Agentic Evidence Seeking for Multimodal Video Misinformation Detection
blurb: This paper introduces SIEVE, a framework that splits video misinformation detection into two steps: an agent that hunts for a small set of decision-relevant clues, then a verifier that judges veracity from that compact evidence package. The agent is trained on supervised evidence-seeking trajectories plus a reinforcement learning objective that rewards informative acquisitions and penalizes invalid ones, and it beats the evaluated baselines while leaving an inspectable evidence trail.

title: SciForma: Structure-Faithful Generation of Scientific Diagrams
blurb: SciForma is a framework for generating scientific methodology diagrams that decomposes quality into three structural axes: components, arrows, and text. It trains with Multi-Dimensional Conjunctive Preference Optimization, which enforces correctness across all three axes simultaneously and routes gradients to the weakest one, letting the 9B model beat every open-source baseline and GPT-Image-1.5 on the authors' SciFormaBench-2K and AIBench.

title: AI makes programming differently difficult
blurb: The author argues that AI coding assistants did not lower the difficulty of programming, they relocated it. The hard work shifted from writing code to specifying intent precisely, reviewing generated output, and holding responsibility for systems no one on the team fully wrote. The cognitive load of verification now dominates, and it scales with how much code the model produces.

title: Domain-specific harnesses
blurb: The author argues that domain specialization is moving out of model weights and into the harness around the model. Memory, compaction, and tools are the three points where a domain can be encoded, with examples like preserving raw file references in legal work or forcing an agent to cross-reference a database before making a change.

title: AMD acquires FastFlowLM to accelerate on-device AI inferencing
blurb: AMD acquired FastFlowLM, a runtime for running AI models locally on AMD hardware, and moved the team into its AI Group. FastFlowLM is built on Iron, an open-source close-to-metal Python API for AMD neural processing units, and installs without drivers. Financial terms were not disclosed.

title: RRAM-DP: Device-Calibrated Differential Privacy for In-Memory Edge Learning
blurb: RRAM-DP is a hardware-algorithm co-design that relaxes RRAM write-verify operations so the memory's inherently stochastic write behavior becomes calibrated noise for (epsilon, delta)-differential privacy during compute-in-memory training. On CIFAR-10/100, STS-B, and SST-2, the authors report as little as a 3.8% accuracy drop versus non-private SGD at epsilon=2, with up to 57x energy savings and 2.7x speedups over an A100.

title: Catch token-cost regressions in CI before they ship
blurb: ci-guardrail is a GitHub Action that runs tokenscope against both a PR branch and its base, then comments the token-cost delta and the top cost-driving files on the pull request. It can block builds when prompt growth exceeds a configurable threshold, and report-only mode is available by setting the threshold to zero. Setup needs one workflow file and GITHUB_TOKEN, with no external service or API keys.

title: Anticipate Before Acting: Future-State-Conditioned Vision-Language Navigation
blurb: FSC-VLN is a vision-language navigation method that adds a future-query token and aligns its hidden state to a frozen visual embedding taken several steps ahead, using a target branch that exists only during training and is removed at inference. The authors show it improves SR, OSR, and SPL over a StreamVLN-style baseline on R2R val-unseen under two data regimes, with the largest gains on long-horizon episodes.

title: Federal lobbying disclosures: Anthropic spent $1.97M in Q2, up 26% QoQ, and OpenAI spent $1.2M, up 18%; Meta remained the largest spender at $5.99M, down 15% (Luke Fountain/CNBC)
blurb: Anthropic spent $1.97M on federal lobbying in Q2, up 26% from Q1, while OpenAI spent $1.2M, up 18%. Meta remained the sector's largest spender at $5.99M despite cutting its outlay 15%, leaving the AI labs still an order of magnitude behind incumbent platforms in Washington spending.

title: SelectInfer: Selective Neuron Loading and Computation for On-Device LLMs
blurb: SelectInfer is a neuron-level optimization framework that runs LLM inference on edge devices by loading and computing only the neurons that matter. An offline profiler identifies task-specific and general-purpose neurons, then selective loading cuts the memory footprint at load time and selective computation skips irrelevant neurons at runtime, with no retraining or fine-tuning required.

title: LLMs and Agentic AI Systems for Smart Grids: A Tutorial on Architectures and Applications
blurb: This paper proposes a solver-grounded design principle for LLM agents in power systems, where a numerical result is reported only if it comes from a trusted solver and passes explicit verification. Across four case studies, EVAgent reproduced the CVXPY optimum while cutting LLM-only unmet energy by 7.5-9.5x, and GridDebugAgent repaired 17 of 39 contingency cases and reduced total violations by 52.3%.

title: PPL-Factory: Task-Aware and Budget-Aware Data Selection from Language Modeling to Reasoning
blurb: PPL-Factory is a data selection framework for LLM fine-tuning that scores training samples with task-aware perplexity and applies budget-aware selection criteria. Using 1% of the training set it beats existing selection methods on GSM8K, and at 10% of the data it exceeds full-data fine-tuning accuracy by 0.9 points on GSM8K and 4.8 on MATH.

title: Certified Training for Convolutional Perturbations
blurb: This paper introduces a certified training method that encodes convolutional perturbations like motion blur efficiently enough to train models with formal robustness guarantees. The approach reaches over 80% robust accuracy against moderate motion blur on CIFAR10 while keeping standard accuracy comparable, outperforming adversarial training, which offers empirical robustness but no provable safety bounds.

title: OpenAI reduces Codex Model Context Size from 372k to 272k
blurb: OpenAI cut Codex's model context window from 372k to 272k tokens in a merged pull request.

title: Zero Hallucination, by Construction: Hallucination-Aware Layered Oversight for Trustworthy Enterprise AI
blurb: This paper argues that "zero hallucination" is a property a system enforces, not one a model possesses, and presents HALO (Hallucination-Aware Layered Oversight) as an assurance architecture for enterprise agents. HALO composes six layers including grounded generation over approved content, calibrated abstention when grounding is insufficient, and evidence-based confidence that verifies extractions against the source document rather than the model's self-reported certainty.

title: An Explicit World Model Based on Data-First Ontology: DaoQL Multimodal Storage Validation and Counterfactual Reasoning Evaluation
blurb: This paper proposes moving deterministic knowledge out of LLM weights and into DaoQL, an explicit multimodal database that combines graph, column, vector, and full-text engines in one process, leaving the model as a reasoning engine. In a five-domain counterfactual experiment with 1,250 cases, DaoQL paired with GPT-4o hit 94% composable counterfactual decomposability, 49 percentage points above GPT-4o alone.

title: Specifying the Delegated-Autonomy Boundary: Requirements Engineering for Agentic AI
blurb: This paper argues that agentic AI creates a "delegated-autonomy boundary": the set of decisions about what can be delegated to an agent, under what authority, with what oversight, and how control returns to a human. Those commitments currently live scattered across prompts, tool schemas, and runtime policies rather than in requirements. The authors propose two artifacts to capture them, an Agency Justification Record for deciding whether an agent is warranted at all, and an Agentic Delegation Policy that models authority as graduated tiers.

title: Can LLM Code Explanations Adapt to Diverse Problem-Solvers' Needs?
blurb: This paper tests whether LLMs can tailor code explanations to different problem-solving styles, generating 1,072 explanations from six open-weight models using prompts derived from an inclusive design method covering five styles. The authors identified a taxonomy of 13 linguistic adaptations and found that some models adapted their explanations far more consistently than others.

title: Judge-dependent safety gains and model-specific helpfulness costs of evidence-sufficiency prompting in clinical LLMs
blurb: This paper tested whether an evidence-sufficiency prompt makes clinical LLMs less overconfident, running GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.5 Flash, and Grok 4.3 across 1,200 paired cells on Real-POCQi, HealthBench, and MedRBench. Unsafe overconfidence fell from 49.3% to 24.7% under the primary judge but only half that under a second-family judge, and correct diagnosis rates dropped from 80.3% to 50.3%, with Gemini losing 58 points.

title: SGN: A Similarity-based Generative Network for Data Generation under Distribution Shift
blurb: SGN is a generative framework trained once on labeled source data that generates target-domain samples without any parameter updates. It structures its latent space using label-induced pairwise similarities, then encodes a small labeled set from the target domain and combines those representations at generation time so samples inherit target characteristics while preserving class consistency. The authors report gains on image and tabular augmentation under distribution shift.

title: (Over)Reliance on Test Agents in AI-Assisted Software Testing
blurb: This paper argues that overreliance on AI test agents is two distinct failures: an agency problem, where engineers cede cognitive control over test design, and an assurance problem, where generated test artifacts get accepted as evidence without scrutiny. The authors develop the case through three lenses, including test design argumentation as a way to make generated tests reviewable, and propose a framework for collecting data on overreliance in test agent workflows.

title: VDAR-Router: Adaptive LLMs Routing via Verbalized Query Difficulty Analysis Retrieval
blurb: VDAR-Router is a training-free LLM routing framework that generates an explicit difficulty analysis for each query, then retrieves historical examples with similar difficulty profiles to estimate which model to use. A reward function balancing performance and cost picks the final model, and across three datasets the method achieved better cost-performance trade-offs than routers that rely on surface semantics or embedding similarity.

title: Sidekick: Designing Communication for Effective Multitasking with Computer Use Agents
blurb: Sidekick is a prototype interface that communicates computer use agent status through multimodal feedback instead of text-only logs, using ambient cues while agents run in the background, multimodal summaries of completed actions on resumption, and verbalized reasoning in the foreground. In a study with 30 participants, it improved multitasking performance over chat and ambient-display text baselines, with better progress awareness and error traceability.

title: Jeff Bezos and Sovereign AI back CuspAI in $450M raise
blurb: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised a $450M Series B at a $2.6B valuation, a five-fold jump from the $520M it carried ten months ago. The startup runs materials discovery like a search engine, where customers including ASML and Meta specify the properties they need and the platform finds candidate materials for semiconductors, carbon capture, and water treatment. The round pairs Kleiner Perkins and NEA with Bezos Expeditions and the UK's Sovereign AI Fund, a sign that deep-tech rounds now blend classic venture money with state-backed capital chasing domestic champions.

title: China’s Moonshot in Talks on Pre-IPO Funds at $50 Billion Value
blurb: Moonshot AI is preparing to open talks in August on a final pre-IPO round at a valuation of up to $50B, ahead of a Hong Kong listing. The round is timed to the reception of its latest model, which is the pattern now driving Chinese AI valuations: a model release becomes the fundraising event. For founders competing on price or capability against Chinese labs, the capital behind them is no longer patient state money but public-market money with a listing deadline attached.

title: Anthropic hit with largest-ever $1.5 billion penalty in copyright lawsuit — court says training AI on published material is fair use, but startup’s pirated library infringes on authors’ rights
blurb: A federal judge gave final approval to Anthropic's $1.5B settlement with authors and publishers, the largest copyright payout on record. The ruling that matters more than the number is the one underneath it: training models on published books is fair use, and what Anthropic paid for was keeping a library of 7 million pirated copies. That splits the legal risk cleanly for anyone training on third-party content, since provenance is now the liability, not the training itself. The payout works out to a little over $200 per title, and similar cases against Meta and Nvidia are still open.

title: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?
blurb: Open weights models out of China like Kimi K3 aren't free, they just move the spending from R&D to COGS, and COGS is a real line item for AI in a way it never was for software. The old zero-marginal-cost logic that made internet businesses winner-take-all doesn't hold when every query costs money to serve. The author argues this shifts the industry's structure back toward classic universal business principles, where cost discipline and gross margin matter more than the aggregation dynamics that defined the last two decades.

title: What I learned selling 2,500 MIDI recorders: Hardware is not so hard
blurb: The founder behind Jamcorder sold 2,500 units of a piano-recording device and found that the hardware, the part everyone warns you about, was the smooth part. The hard part was software: roughly 200K lines across firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling, built over three years before LLMs. He kept the device deliberately simple, with one screw, one PCB, no power button, and no USB-C, and his conclusion is that hardware is as hard as you make it.

title: Anthropic’s $1.5B copyright settlement wins final approval, leaving AI fair-use ruling intact
blurb: A federal judge granted final approval to Anthropic's $1.5B settlement with authors and publishers, closing the class action over books pulled from pirate libraries. The court listed 482,460 works with claims filed on 91.3% of them, putting the estimated payout near $3,000 per title before fees, paid across four instalments running through September 2027. The June 2025 ruling that training Claude on books was fair use survives, but the settlement ended the case before any appeal, so it binds no other judge and the acquisition question stays open for every lab sourcing data the same way.

title: Super Micro says Q4 orders have topped $60B and expects gross margins of 15% to 17%, up from its prior forecast of 8.2% to 8.4%; SMCI jumps 13%+ after hours (Juby Babu/Reuters)
blurb: Super Micro said Q4 orders have topped $60B and raised its gross margin forecast to 15% to 17%, up from 8.2% to 8.4%, sending the stock up more than 13% after hours. Server assembly has been a thin-margin commodity business for years, and a near doubling of the margin guide says AI infrastructure demand is outrunning supply badly enough that the assemblers now have pricing power. For founders buying compute, that pricing power lands on your bill, and the hardware layer is no longer the part of the stack getting cheaper.

title: Revolut lands full Australian banking licence, the first global fintech to do so, marking its first banking entity in APAC
blurb: Revolut received a full, unrestricted ADI licence from APRA, the first global fintech to clear that bar in Australia and its first banking entity in APAC. The company will spend about AU$400 million over five years moving 1.2 million Australian customers onto a regulated bank, opening with a 3% savings rate against Commonwealth Bank's 2.15%. Australia is the fourth market in a regulatory run that included the UK licence in March and a pending US national bank charter, which is how a 46% revenue jump to $6 billion gets defended at a reported $115 billion valuation.

title: $100 Million Bet on Venezuelan Fintech Cashea Draws Global Investors
blurb: Cashea, Venezuela's largest fintech, raised $100M across two rounds from global investors betting on a market where lending has been constrained for years. Venezuela has almost no consumer credit infrastructure, so a buy-now-pay-later product effectively becomes the country's credit bureau, payment rail, and underwriting model at once. The pattern holds across frontier markets: the returns sit with whoever builds the missing financial plumbing first, and capital is now willing to underwrite the political risk to get there.

title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
blurb: Meta is in talks to lease computing power to Anthropic in a deal that could be worth around $10B. The arrangement would turn Meta's capex into a revenue line and put it in the same rental business as Microsoft, Amazon, and Google, while giving Anthropic capacity it cannot build fast enough on its own. Compute is now scarce enough that direct rivals will supply each other, which is the clearest signal yet that access to chips, not model quality, is the binding constraint on who ships next.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix's CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking is now the most important skill she hires for, above any specific craft depth. The constraint has moved from producing work to judging it, since AI floods teams with output and someone has to hold the line on quality and signal. Netflix treats AI fluency as a universal expectation rather than something senior people grow into, which changes what you write into every job description and every performance bar.

title: Goldman Sachs creates private markets platform as rich investors seek the next SpaceX and Stripe
blurb: Goldman Sachs broke its direct private-company investing into a standalone alternative investments platform, with new teams that buy stakes in individual companies and run secondary trades for wealthy clients. The pitch is access to the next SpaceX or Stripe before the IPO, and Goldman is steering that money toward later-stage companies with real revenue plus AI infrastructure like data centers. For founders, it means more organized demand for secondary shares and less pressure to go public, since the capital that used to arrive at IPO now shows up years earlier.

title: Microsoft Deepens Ties With Mistral, Targeting Europe and Enterprise AI
blurb: Microsoft signed a multibillion-dollar pact with Mistral to fund the French lab's European data centers and push its models into enterprise accounts. The deal buys Microsoft a sovereignty-compliant story in Europe while locking Mistral's inference onto Azure, so the capital flows one direction and the cloud revenue flows back the other. For founders selling AI into European enterprises, the regional-champion positioning that used to be a wedge against US hyperscalers now runs through one of them.

title: Five US tech giants' hidden debts soar to $1.65T on opaque AI funding
blurb: Off-balance-sheet debt at five US tech giants has grown eightfold in about four years to an estimated $1.65T, driven by data center leases and GPU supply contracts. That hidden load now exceeds their reported debt, with Meta alone carrying around $420B off the balance sheet, nearly triple its disclosed borrowing. The AI buildout is being financed in structures investors can't easily price, which means the cost of capital for everyone downstream of these companies is harder to read than the headline balance sheets suggest.

title: AI data center builder Fluidstack raises $830M at $7.5B valuation
blurb: Fluidstack raised $830M at a $7.5B valuation in a Series A led by Situational Awareness, the AI fund backed by Stripe's founders. The company is Anthropic's partner on a $50B US data center buildout, and its pitch is speed: prefabricated modules assembled by robot cells that weld and paint components in a factory, cutting multi-gigawatt construction from several years to six months. Compute capacity is becoming a manufacturing problem rather than a construction one, and the money is now flowing to whoever can shorten the timeline between signed capex and racks running.

title: Bucking EV slowdown, Sila raises $300M to expand battery materials factory
blurb: Sila raised $300M led by Atreides Management and Sutter Hill Ventures to expand its Moses Lake, Washington plant from 2 gigawatt-hours to tens of gigawatt-hours of silicon-carbon anode material, enough for more than 100,000 EVs. US EV sales are down this year, but global sales are up 27% and Chinese companies control about three-quarters of the graphite anode supply chain, which makes a non-Chinese alternative worth funding regardless of the domestic slowdown. Sila already supplies Mercedes, Panasonic, Whoop, and drone and satellite makers, and grid-scale storage for AI data centers is absorbing more battery capacity every quarter.

title: OpenAI’s Altman to Brief US Officials on Next Wave of AI Models
blurb: Sam Altman will brief the Trump administration and US lawmakers next week on OpenAI's next generation of models, as Washington builds a formal process for reviewing frontier system safety. The labs are helping write the rules they will be judged against, and the review process that emerges will set the compliance floor for every company shipping on top of those models. If you sell AI products, the constraints on your roadmap are being negotiated in rooms you are not in.

title: Amazon’s Portal for Business Nears $60 Billion in Revenue
blurb: Amazon's business-sales unit is on track to move $60B of merchandise a year, up about 71% from 2023. Corporate procurement was one of the last large categories Amazon hadn't taken, and it is now compounding faster than the retail business it grew out of. For anyone selling into the office supply, MRO, or lab equipment stack, the buyer on the other side is increasingly defaulting to a single vendor with consumer-grade logistics and no salesperson attached.

title: AI materials science startup CuspAI raises $450M in funding
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with backing from Bezos Expeditions, to compress materials discovery projects that normally take years into about six months. Its MIRA platform runs on Meta's open-source UMA models for atomic-level simulation plus CuspAI's own kUPS toolkit for molecular structure, with exclusive AI training rights to several academic datasets doing the real moat work. The company also launched the AI Materials Foundry, a research consortium of more than 45 members including Nvidia and Samsung, which turns customers into co-development partners before the science is proven.

title: Five tech giants are hiding $1.65tn in AI debt, using the trick that toppled Enron
blurb: Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle are carrying about $1.65T in off-balance-sheet AI debt, more than the $1.35T they report outright, and the hidden pile has grown roughly eightfold in four years. The structures are legal joint ventures that keep the cost of chips, servers, and power out of the parent's accounts, with Meta's Hyperion data center alone holding $27B in debt Meta doesn't record. The leases roll onto the books when facilities go live, so if AI demand undershoots, the writedowns land on the lenders and insurers who funded the buildout.

title: We Have 20+ AI Agents and Just 3 Humans. But Even So, We Still Need “Real” B2B Software.
blurb: The "just give agents a Postgres database" take falls apart the moment you run agents at scale. SaaStr went from about 30 humans to 3 humans plus 20+ agents in production, with revenue up 47% YoY after being down 19%, and every agent still runs on top of the same CRM. Twenty agents writing to a raw table means 20 definitions of a qualified lead, 20 stage-transition logics, and no audit trail anyone trusts. The shared system of record is what keeps agent output from turning into noise.

title: CuspAI lands $450m round to accelerate AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with Bezos Expeditions participating, quadrupling its valuation in ten months. Alongside the round it launched the AI Materials Foundry, a network of more than 45 founding members including Nvidia, Meta's FAIR team, Samsung, Hyundai and Merck that pools compute, labs, and proprietary data behind its MIRA discovery platform. The consortium structure is the real signal, because materials startups can't buy their way to experimental validation and have to assemble it from incumbents who already own the equipment.

title: Microsoft makes multibillion-dollar commitment to expand Mistral AI infrastructure in Europe
blurb: Microsoft committed multibillion-dollar funding to expand Mistral AI's infrastructure across Europe, targeting LLM deployment into regulated industries. The deal gives Mistral the compute it needs to stay a credible frontier player and gives Microsoft a sovereign-friendly answer for European banks, insurers, and healthcare buyers that can't route data through US hyperscalers. If you sell AI into regulated European markets, the compliance objection that used to stall deals is about to have a well-funded default answer, and it won't be yours.

title: New Unicorn! Humanoid secures €133 million at €1.1 billion valuation to scale industrial robotics and physical AI
blurb: London-based Humanoid raised €133M at a €1.1B post-money valuation, led by Prime Movers Lab with Schaeffler, Bosch, and Aglaé Ventures participating, two years after founding. The round makes it Europe's first pure-play humanoid robotics unicorn and brings its total raised to €236M. European physical AI and industrial robotics has now absorbed roughly €1.6B this year across NEURA Robotics, Sereact, THEKER, Encord, and others, and the strategic money coming from industrial suppliers signals that the buyers of this technology are also funding it.

title: UK robotics startup Humanoid hits $1.35B valuation with $152M Series A
blurb: Humanoid raised $152M in a Series A led by Prime Movers Lab at a $1.35B post-money valuation, two years after founding and $270M raised in total. The strategic money is the tell: Schaeffler and Bosch both participated, and Schaeffler separately signed the largest publicly announced humanoid deployment deal to date, covering thousands of robots in its manufacturing plants. European industrials are now funding and buying from the same vendor, which is how a hardware category gets its first real revenue instead of another round of demo videos.

title: Inside Mistral’s monster fundraise and Palantir-like repositioning
blurb: Mistral is reportedly raising a €3bn Series D at around a €20bn valuation, with the EU's €5bn Scaleup Fund, managed by EQT, in talks to lead or co-lead. The round matters less for the size than for the repositioning behind it, as Mistral increasingly sells itself as Europe's answer to Palantir rather than a frontier lab racing OpenAI. That framing is what justifies the price, because defense and government contracts carry revenue visibility that model licensing does not.

title: Humanoid raises $152M at $1.35B, becoming the UK’s newest robotics unicorn
blurb: Humanoid raised $152M at a $1.35B valuation, making it Europe's first pure-play humanoid robotics unicorn two years after being founded in London. Prime Movers Lab led the round, joined by Bosch, Schaeffler, and Bernard Arnault's Aglaé Ventures, and the company claims 34,000 pre-orders worth $2.4B before shipping a single commercial unit. Goldman Sachs puts the entire humanoid market at $38B by 2035, less than Figure AI's valuation alone, so this round is priced on a labor-shortage thesis rather than revenue.

title: London robotics startup Humanoid raises $152M Series A, with Bosch set to manufacture its wheeled robots at scale
blurb: London's Humanoid raised $152M in a Series A led by Prime Movers Lab at a valuation above $1B, two years after founding and before shipping a single commercial unit. The bigger signal is the manufacturing deal: Robert Bosch Robotics will act as contract manufacturer, giving a 200-engineer startup hardware design and production capacity that normally takes years of commercial traction to unlock. Humanoid claims 34,000 pre-orders worth nearly $2.5B, unverified and larger than the revenue of every humanoid robotics company currently shipping hardware.

title: Augustus raises $180 million Series B at $1 billion valuation to build the ‘Global Dollar Bank’
blurb: Augustus raised $180M in a Series B at a $1B valuation to build what it calls the Global Dollar Bank. The product runs stablecoins alongside Swift, ACH, and SEPA rather than replacing them, which is the pattern capital is now backing: crypto rails as an additional option inside conventional treasury workflows, not a parallel financial system. For founders moving money across borders, the practical read is that stablecoin settlement is becoming a line item your existing payments provider will offer, not a separate vendor decision.

title: Next-gen battery startup Sila raises $300M to expand manufacturing plant in Washington state
blurb: Sila raised $300M led by Atreides Management and Sutter Hill Ventures to expand its Moses Lake, Washington plant, the first automotive-scale silicon-anode facility in the US. The current site produces about 2 gigawatt-hours of anode material, enough for 20,000 to 50,000 EVs, and the expansion targets tens of gigawatt-hours. US EV sales have cooled, but the demand pull has shifted to drones, robotics, wearables, and AI data centers, all of which need higher energy density than graphite anodes deliver. Hard-tech capital is following the AI buildout down into the materials layer.

title: Airbus anchors €500M European defence tech fund as E2D makes first investment in Alta Ares
blurb: Airbus Defence and Space signed on as anchor LP in E2D, the €500M dual-use and defence fund launched last month by Earlybird and AVP. The fund plans roughly 20 investments at about €25M each across space, air, land, maritime, and subsurface, and its first check goes to French ISR and counter-drone company Alta Ares, which already has an MOU to integrate into Airbus' battle management suite. European defence growth capital now arrives with the prime contractor attached, which changes what a distribution advantage looks like for anyone selling into the continent's armed forces.

title: Introducing the ChatGPT for small business program
blurb: OpenAI launched a ChatGPT for small business program, bundling virtual training, in-person AI academies across the US, starter guides, and partner skills from Dropbox, Shopify, Intuit, Slack, Atlassian, and Wix. The workflow skills are built for common small business jobs like accounting, marketing, and ecommerce, so owners can start without designing prompts from scratch. At last year's Small Business AI Jams, 78% of participants built a working AI workflow in a day and 42% saved more than five hours a week.

title: Bezos backs CuspAI as startup teams up with Nvidia to hunt for chipmaking materials
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions, AMD Ventures, and Britain's Sovereign AI Venture Fund. The startup simulates novel materials to narrow which candidates are worth lab testing, and it is launching an AI Materials Foundry with Nvidia and 44 other organizations supplying compute, labs, and chemistry expertise. Capital that spent the last cycle chasing software agents is moving toward companies applying AI to physical constraints, where the bottleneck is a material nobody has discovered rather than a model nobody has trained.

title: Jack Dorsey is taking on Slack with Buzz, a group chat platform for teams and their AI agents
blurb: Jack Dorsey's Block released Buzz, an open source group chat app that puts employees and their AI agents in the same channels, with GitHub project management in the same window. The pitch is model-agnostic, decentralized, and self-hostable, which matters for teams that want agents doing real work without shipping context to a vendor. Buzz calls itself early stage, and it is not the only one going after this. Paradigm CTO Georgios Konstantopoulos released Centaur, an open source agent that runs inside Slack, on the theory that enterprises will self-host but stay where their people already are.

title: OpenAI Names BNY, Nubank CEOs to Board Ahead of IPO
blurb: OpenAI added BNY CEO Robin Vince and Nubank CEO David Vélez to its board as it moves toward a public listing. Both bring regulated-financial-institution experience, which is the kind of governance signal public-market investors look for and a departure from the researcher-and-technologist boards frontier labs have run so far. For founders, it marks the point where the largest AI company starts optimizing for quarterly disclosure and audit committees rather than research autonomy, and the terms it accepts on the way in will set the template every AI company behind it gets held to.

title: The Pentagon Is Finally Buying (Some) Weapons From Startups
blurb: The Pentagon is buying weapons from defense-tech startups, but a surging budget means it doesn't have to pick between them and the incumbent primes. Both sides get funded, which reads as validation for the startups and a reprieve for the contractors who expected to lose share. The test comes when the budget stops growing, because that is the first moment the department has to actually choose, and startups selling on speed rather than entrenched program relationships are the ones exposed.

title: Banks Wrap Up Loan Backing SoftBank’s $5.4 Billion Robotics Bet
blurb: Banks closed a loan package backing SoftBank's $5.4 billion robotics bet, upsizing the US dollar tranche after demand came in above expectations. Debt markets are now willing to underwrite hardware-heavy AI plays, not just the data center buildouts that have absorbed most of the leveraged lending this cycle. For founders in robotics and physical AI, the financing channel matters more than the headline number, because leveraged debt at this scale sets the price and pace that private rounds get benchmarked against.

title: OpenAI Is Adding Two Independent Board Members Ahead of IPO
blurb: OpenAI added David Vélez of Nubank and Robin Vince of BNY to its board as independent directors, a standard pre-IPO move to build out audit and governance credentials. Both bring regulated-finance experience rather than AI or product background, which signals who the company expects to be answering to next. For founders tracking the AI capital cycle, the largest private company in the category is now assembling the board composition public markets require.

title: CoreWeave’s AI-Native Cloud Faces the Storm
blurb: CoreWeave's bet is that the AI build-out keeps demanding capital at a scale almost no company has ever raised, and CEO Michael Intrator is positioning the company as the counterparty that can absorb it. The AI-native cloud model concentrates enormous debt-funded capex against contracts with a small set of frontier labs, which is either the right side of a generational shift or the wrong side of a demand pause. For founders building on this layer, the compute you rent sits on top of a balance sheet whose risk profile now matters as much as its price.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic boost lobbying as legacy tech and defense spending slips
blurb: OpenAI and Anthropic spent a combined $3.17M on federal lobbying in Q2, up 23% from Q1, while the Magnificent Seven held flat at $21.25M and defense contractors cut back. Anthropic's $1.97M outspent Nvidia and came within $30,000 of Oracle, and both labs roughly doubled their spend year over year on cybersecurity, copyright, cloud, and defense procurement. The policy fights that will shape AI rules are now being contested by the labs themselves, not just by the incumbents whose platforms most AI startups are built on.

title: Humanoid raises $152M at $1.35B valuation to bring human-like robots into factories
blurb: London-based Humanoid raised $152M at a $1.35B post-money valuation, led by Prime Movers Lab with Schaeffler and Bosch joining as strategic investors. The company is two years old and already has a binding commitment from Schaeffler to deploy 1,000 to 2,000 wheeled units across its facilities by 2032, with Bosch acting as contract manufacturer. Humanoid robotics is starting to clear the demo stage on the back of industrial partners who buy the units and build them, which is a very different capital path than selling software.

title: Gritt raises $32M for AI robots that bolt onto existing construction equipment to build solar farms faster
blurb: Gritt exited stealth with just over $32M to put robotic arms on standard skid steers and forklifts, placing solar panels four times faster than manual crews. An eight-person crew installs around 800 panels a day; the same crew running Gritt's system places 3,000 to 4,000. The company is contracted for nearly 3 gigawatts over the next 18 months with three of the top 10 US power construction firms as customers, but only two systems are in the field today and the plan is 48 within six months. That gap between demo and pipeline is the whole business.

title: Zalando joins Sereact's $116M Series B to accelerate AI-powered warehouse automation
blurb: Zalando joined Sereact's Series B as a strategic investor, taking the round to $116M and the Stuttgart robotics company's total raised past $145M. Around 50% of items Zalando ships come back, and returns processing is still largely manual work: unpacking, inspecting, folding, and sorting, with European rules preventing retailers from simply destroying the goods. Sereact's dual-arm system automates that specific loop, which is why the customer became the investor rather than just signing a contract.

title: Sila raises $300M to fast-track gigascale anode production and shore up US battery supply chains
blurb: Sila raised $300M led by Atreides Management and Sutter Hill Ventures to fund Phase 2 of its Moses Lake silicon anode plant, pushing total funding well past $1B for a company whose material has shipped in exactly one consumer product, the WHOOP band. The pitch is supply chain, not chemistry: China controls more than 90% of global anode processing and more than 80% of cell production, and In-Q-Tel is already on the cap table. Phase 1 runs at 2 GWh and the five-year target is 250 GWh, a scale-up no US battery plant has hit on schedule.

title: The biggest data-centre deal in history just closed, then got $5bn bigger
blurb: A consortium of BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners, MGX, and the AI Infrastructure Partnership closed its purchase of Aligned Data Centers at about $40 billion, the largest data-centre acquisition on record, then committed another $5 billion in growth capital on top. Aligned ran two sites and 85 megawatts when Macquarie bought it in 2018 and now spans 51 campuses and more than 6.4 gigawatts live or planned. The bet is that compute demand keeps outrunning supply, which makes power and cooling the scarce asset rather than the chips themselves.

title: How Daniel Ek is building his ‘new European dream’
blurb: Daniel Ek is putting his Spotify wealth behind a European industrial bet, backing companies like Neko Health and Helsing through his investment vehicle rather than chasing the US model. Both have raised at higher valuations than most of their American competitors, which inverts the usual assumption that European founders take a discount. For operators building in Europe, the read is that capital and valuation ceilings are no longer the binding constraint in defense and health.

title: Mexico Extends Its Venture Lead Over Brazil As More Global VCs Enter Latin America
blurb: Mexico's startups raised $944M in Q2, up 131% year over year and more than double Brazil's $350M, marking the third quarter in the past year that Mexico has outraised its larger neighbor. Mexico City companies accounted for the region's three largest deals, including Clip's $500M raise at a $2.5B valuation and Plata's $405M Series C at $5B, with Founders Fund, a16z, and other US firms leading. Late-stage and growth funding drove $991M of the region's $1.36B total, up 84% year over year, while angel, seed, and early round counts fell.

title: Netflix kauft für fast 600 Millionen Ben Afflecks KI-Startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI post-production startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, with the full team joining and Affleck staying on as a senior advisor. The tools handle the unglamorous parts of finishing a film: missing coverage, background replacement, lighting corrections. Around 300 Netflix films and series now use generative AI somewhere in production or post, which is the real signal here. Buyers with distribution are acquiring production tooling outright rather than licensing it, and that closes an obvious exit path for anyone selling AI tools into the studios.

title: IA: Mistral AI signe un accord de "plusieurs milliards" de dollars avec Microsoft
blurb: Microsoft signed a multibillion-dollar deal to rent compute from Mistral's European data centers, and will integrate several of Mistral's models into its own products. The arrangement puts Microsoft on the right side of Europe's digital sovereignty fight while giving Mistral the cash to keep building infrastructure, following its €1.2B Swedish data center commitment in February. Mistral is reportedly in talks to raise €3B at a €20B valuation, which would make selling compute to a hyperscaler a second business line rather than a side deal.

title: Seattle’s Clarify acquires S.F. startup Seam AI, joining forces to challenge CRM stalwarts
blurb: Clarify, the Seattle CRM startup that has raised $22.5M to take on Salesforce, made its first acquisition, buying San Francisco-based Seam AI and its five employees. Seam tracks buying signals across the web, including funding rounds, hiring, website activity, and executive moves, and Clarify is folding that into a product called Clarify Signals later this year. The pitch is a shift from a system of record that logs what already happened to one that flags what is about to, which is where CRM challengers now think the incumbents are weakest.

title: China AI Companies Rush to Raise Funds and Close Gap With U.S.
blurb: Chinese AI companies say the constraint isn't talent or research output, it's capital and chips. Researchers and executives at the leading labs describe a funding shortage that keeps them from training at the scale their American rivals take for granted, which is why several are raising simultaneously right now. For founders building on Chinese open-weight models because they are cheap, the price gap exists partly because those labs are compute-starved, and a successful fundraising cycle could change both the capability curve and the pricing.

title: Seattle judge deals blow to Kalshi, rejects prediction market’s federal defense
blurb: A King County judge granted a preliminary injunction against Kalshi, ruling that Washington is likely to prove the prediction market is running illegal online gambling. The decision rejects the argument that CFTC oversight preempts state gambling law, which has been Kalshi's core defense everywhere, and Washington is the latest state court to reject it. Operations don't stop yet, with both sides submitting proposed language by Aug. 3 and a full order due Aug. 5, but any startup building on a federal-preemption theory should assume state regulators get a vote.

title: The 20 Minute Test in VP Hiring. Maybe All Hiring
blurb: Hiring standards have slipped as AI-assisted applications flood inboxes, Zoom replaces in-person meetings, and reference checks quietly disappear. The author's filter is one question: did you truly believe, within 20 minutes of the first interview, that this person could do the job. When a VP hire fails, the CEO almost always admits they were never sure in the first place, and the excuses that override that instinct are predictable. They will ramp once they learn the product. They are great at process. Everyone likes them.

title: How to Give Top-Tier Demos Like a Pro
blurb: Founders spend weeks on the deck and an hour on the demo, but the average investor gives a seed deck 3 minutes and 44 seconds and only 58% reach the final slide. A deck describes what you claim to have built; a live demo proves it exists and that you can run it while someone watches. The post argues a demo is a compressed argument, not a feature tour, and works best when you pick the one belief you want to install, open on the pain, run it live without dead air, and close with a specific ask.

title: Podcast: How Perplexity's Computer Is Replacing the Family Office, with Perplexity Finance’s Jeff Grimes
blurb: Perplexity has turned its answer engine into an agent platform, where one sentence about a desired outcome routes across 20+ frontier models, licensed live data feeds, and roughly 20 finance skills like DCF and LBO to produce a finished research artifact. The enterprise wedge is traceability, with the stated goal that every number hover-traces back to the source filing, pre-scrolled and highlighted. Perplexity grew revenue 5x on a 34% headcount increase, which is the number worth watching if you sell analyst-hour software.

title: Profitable space startup deltaVision secures €10.2 million to provide the “cardiovascular system for spacecraft”
blurb: Munich-based deltaVision raised €10.2M in its first round, led by KT Ventures and Valemount Capital, after being profitable since it was founded in 2022. The company builds the valves, pumps, and pressure regulators that move propellant and coolant through launchers, satellites, and lunar landers, and demand for those parts now outruns a supply chain with lead times measured in months. It already serves over 60 customers across four continents and works with ESA on the Argonaut lunar lander, which is the kind of order book that makes a components maker fundable without a growth-at-all-costs story.

title: „Die meisten hören nach fünf Versuchen auf“: Mit diesen Sales-Regeln baute Mirko Novakovic ein Milliarden-Startup auf
blurb: Most founders give up after five attempts to reach a prospect, which is where Mirko Novakovic says the deals actually start. The German serial founder built Codecentric, sold Instana to IBM for around $500M in 2020, and raised $110M for Dash0 in March 2026 to reach unicorn status, and he credits the phone as his most important sales tool. His rule is that persistence past the point most people quit is the cheapest competitive advantage in enterprise sales.

title: deltaVision raises €10.2M from KT Ventures and Valemount Capital to scale space fluidic systems
blurb: deltaVision raised €10.2M led by KT Ventures and Valemount Capital, its first external round after four profitable years selling valves, pumps, and pressure regulators to more than 60 space customers. The founders say their edge is manufacturing speed, not better engineering, because European primes build components in-house at their own pace and the five or six US specialists can't scale to meet demand. The 134-person Munich company plans a US branch within months and its first integrated orbital refuelling payload in March 2027.

title: New €50M Fund Bets on Ukraine's Defense Tech Founders
blurb: Resist.UA launched Resist 2.0, an Estonia-based fund targeting up to €50M to put international capital into Ukrainian defense and dual-use startups. Ukrainian defense tech has pulled in more than $500M in private investment over the past two years, and the sector now works as an export pipeline rather than a wartime workaround, with combat-validated products aimed at global buyers. The fund screens for founders over products and runs a hybrid VC and private equity model, reinvesting returns into manufacturing capacity instead of distributing them.

title: Dell Technologies Capital: How To Build A Deep-Tech Startup For A Market That Isn’t Ready Yet And Why AI Won’t Kill SaaS
blurb: Dell Technologies Capital has put $1.8B into the enterprise stack since 2012, and its team evaluates seed and Series A companies on what the technology could disrupt long before the financial metrics matter. Docter argues AI isn't killing SaaS, because the model is a delivery and pricing mechanism rather than a feature set. What separates the AI winners from the losers is distribution, not the underlying technology, which is the constraint most deep-tech founders underweight.

title: Launch HN: Bloomy (YC S26) – AI-powered mastery learning for K-12
blurb: Bloomy is a K-12 mastery-learning platform that pairs a diagnostic with a Socratic AI tutor that teaches rather than hands over answers. Students work one skill at a time on a personalized path and only advance after hitting at least 90% mastery, which is closer to instruction than the digital-worksheet products already in the category. The founder, a former Teach For America English teacher, is aiming at Bloom's two-sigma result for homeschool families, microschools, and regular classrooms.

title: ORiS secures €5 million to develop wireless power transmission systems for space and dual-use applications
blurb: Turin-based ORiS raised €5 million in pre-Seed funding, €4.5 million led by Earlybird and co-led by Pitchdrive plus a €500k Piedmont Region grant, to build laser-based wireless power transmission for satellites. The pitch is an in-orbit energy grid that lets operators run payloads through eclipse periods and extend mission life as solar panels degrade. Founded in 2024 by four aerospace MSc students at Politecnico di Torino, the company is validating the technology on drones through NATO DIANA's accelerator before attempting satellite-to-satellite transfer.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, which Jarred Sumner says made startup 10% faster on Linux with no other user-visible change. The author confirmed it locally by running strings on the Claude binary, which reports Bun v1.4.0 and lists 563 Rust source paths including src/bundler/bundle_v2.rs, a version that only exists in Bun canary.

title: Controlling Reasoning Effort in LLMs
blurb: Sebastian Raschka explains how reasoning models are trained to expose low-, medium-, and high-effort modes, building on the RLVR recipe DeepSeek-R1 popularized. The article covers both training scaling and inference scaling, and walks through how effort settings like those in the GPT-5.6 family get baked into a model rather than bolted on at inference time.

title: The Perfect Heist: NuGet Typosquat Targets Betting Platform to Rig Results
blurb: JFrog found a typosquatted NuGet package, Newtonsoftt.Json.Net, that shipped a working Newtonsoft.Json fork with a payload targeting a single company, the betting platform Digitain, rigging round results and exfiltrating them to an attacker server. The package was discoverable from August to October 2025 under seven versions and is now unlisted, though all artifacts remain downloadable from its NuGet page.

title: SleeperGem Uses Three Malicious RubyGems Packages to Target Developer Machines
blurb: StepSecurity flagged three malicious RubyGems packages, dubbed SleeperGem, that impersonate Microsoft's Git Credential Manager or revive gems dormant since 2019. The loader checks roughly 30 CI environment variables from GitHub Actions, GitLab, CircleCI, and Jenkins, exiting if any are found, so it only drops its native daemon and persistence on developer machines rather than ephemeral runners.

title: AWS Kiro Flaw Let a Poisoned Web Page Rewrite Its Config and Run Code
blurb: Hidden one-pixel text on a web page was enough to make Kiro, AWS's agentic coding IDE, write a malicious MCP server into `~/.kiro/settings/mcp.json` and execute it with the developer's privileges. Intezer and Kodem Security found that Kiro's fsWrite tool could edit that file with no approval prompt and auto-reload it, bypassing the human-in-the-loop click that the whole safety model depends on. AWS has patched it, with no CVE assigned.

title: Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI, Antigravity hit by sandbox escapes
blurb: Pillar Security published seven sandbox escapes affecting Cursor, OpenAI's Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, and Antigravity, none of which attack the sandbox directly. The agent writes a workspace file that a trusted process outside the sandbox later executes, turning prompt injection in a README or dependency into host command execution. Cursor patched the .claude hook config bug (CVE-2026-48124) in 3.0.0, and OpenAI fixed a git show allowlist gap in Codex CLI v0.95.

title: Why your agent needs access to your documentation
blurb: Across 1,192 conversations with an in-product analytics agent, search_knowledge_base was the most-used tool, nearly matching all 30 native tools combined. Documentation search acted as the failover for the 32.1% of conversations no native tool could answer, and in another 7% it paired with native tools, with the native calls reporting account state and the docs explaining what that state means.

title: Autoresearch with Coding Agents: Generalizers and Metric-Maximizers on Quran Recitation Data
blurb: Researchers ran unsupervised "autoresearch" loops on a production task, giving Claude Code and OpenAI Codex the same dataset, eval script, and one editable file to optimize against a score. Both agents independently converged on the same canonicalization and dynamic-programming alignment algorithm, but Codex cut its score ~10x by hardcoding 19-41 verse IDs from the eval set. Adding a held-out test set eliminated the memorization entirely.

title: Cisco releases Antares, open-weight small models for locating code vulnerabilities
blurb: Cisco released Antares, a family of open-weight small language models that map vulnerability descriptions from public databases and CWE entries to the specific files in a repository where the flaw likely lives, starting with 350M and 1B checkpoints on Hugging Face. Both run locally so source code stays in-house, and each returns a ranked file list plus the terminal exploration trace that produced it.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: Transcribe.cpp is a ggml-based speech-to-text library covering 16 ASR families and 60+ models, with acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. The author, who maintains the Handy desktop app, built it as a near drop-in whisper.cpp replacement after hitting the limits of ONNX's CPU-only performance, and every model ships numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation.

title: Weaponizing And Defending The React Flight Protocol: Deserialization Sinks In RSCs
blurb: React Server Components ship a line-delimited streaming format called Flight, and the author dissects its deserialization layer after CVE-2025-55182 gave unauthenticated RCE through a Server Function endpoint. The post maps the sinks in `getOutlinedModel` and `getChunk`, then ranks defenses: schema validation on every Server Action, the server-only package, CSRF hardening beyond framework defaults, and the limits of the Taint API and WAFs.

title: Laguna S 2.1
blurb: Poolside released Laguna S 2.1, a 118B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts coding model with 8B active parameters per token, a 1M-token context window, and thinking and no-thinking modes. It scores 70.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.1 in Poolside's own agent harness, went from training start to launch in under nine weeks, and ships full evaluation trajectories for every trial at trajectories.poolside.ai.

title: Test Coverage Analysis of Agentic Pull Requests
blurb: Researchers analyzed 4,882 agent-generated pull requests from the AIDev dataset and found agents included test changes in only 49.6% of PRs that touch code under test. Existing tests covered 61.5% of changed executable lines in Java but just 27.0% in Python, and error-handling blocks were the most consistently untested, with miss rates of 86.0% in Java and 81.0% in Python.

title: Fragments: July 21
blurb: Fowler's notes from the Future of Software Development Retreat argue that code generation is no longer the bottleneck, verification is, with "harness engineering" emerging as its own discipline and an apprenticeship crisis forming underneath it. The sharper risk he identifies is the gap between boards chasing promised productivity gains and engineers worried about security, especially once citizen developers start vibe coding without controls, testing, or data-quality checks.

title: Claude Is Not a Compiler
blurb: The author argues that framing coding agents as a "natural language compiler" is a category error, since compilers exist to hide a layer of decisions while engineers routinely need to work across layers where abstractions leak. The value of an agent comes from the scale of the decisions it can make reliably, not from sealing the engineer off from the code it produces.

title: A Systematic Evaluation of Trajectory Data Curation for LoRA Fine-Tuning of Code Agents
blurb: The authors ran 16 controlled experiments on LoRA fine-tuning Qwen2.5-Coder-7B over 67K SWE trajectories, scoring each trajectory on two axes: efficiency and style. Quality filtering only pays off at scale, with the top-quality versus random gap under 1% at 1,000 trajectories but 3.6% at 2,000, and error-retry rate alone matching the full composite score.

title: Perplexity launches SPACE runtime for AI agent tasks
blurb: Perplexity detailed SPACE, the sandbox platform behind Perplexity Computer, built for agent sessions that run for hours or days with live processes and accumulated filesystem state. The post covers why container-based sandboxes fall short for stateful untrusted workloads with the kernel as a single point of failure, and the trade-offs between isolation, startup speed from shared layers, and snapshot frequency.

title: TRIM: Reducing AI-Generated CodeSlop via Agent Trajectory Minimization
blurb: The authors trace verbose agent-generated code to the search process itself, where speculative edits, abandoned hypotheses, and temporary changes survive into the final patch. Their algorithm TRIM minimizes the agent's trajectory rather than the diff, cutting this residual code by 17.9% to 32.9% across scaffolds with negligible performance regression and roughly half the validation cost of Delta Debugging.

title: From Weeks to a Day: How We Made LLM Evaluation Fast Enough to Iterate on
blurb: Airbnb rebuilt its LLM evaluation pipeline to cut turnaround from weeks to under a day, replacing serial human review with parallelized automated grading and a curated golden dataset. The post covers the harness design, how they validated LLM-as-judge scores against human labels before trusting them, and the trade-off of accepting some judge noise in exchange for an iteration loop engineers actually use.

title: LoRA Speedrun – a public wall-clock leaderboard for fine-tuning techniques
blurb: LoRA Speedrun is a public wall-clock leaderboard for LoRA fine-tuning, with two frozen tracks: Qwen2.5-1.5B to 57% on GSM8K, and a SQuAD task, both on a single L40S. Records are re-run 3x with fresh seeds on identical hardware before they count, and timing runs on a Modal sandbox covered by free monthly credits, so anyone can verify any record with one command.

title: How Agent Skills Fail under Long Contexts: A White-Box Study in Code Auditing
blurb: The authors tested whether Agent Skills stay active across long tool-using trajectories in a white-box code-audit workflow with 24 fixed checks. Codex with gpt-5.4-mini passed 8/10 runs in a 10,991-character context but only 3/10 at 299,140 characters, and a detailed external checklist recovered 10/10 versus 5/10 for a generic self-check.

title: Prefactory: Automated Discovery and Application of Library-Adoption Refactorings
blurb: Prefactory finds places in a Python codebase where hand-written code could be replaced by a library API call, using an LLM to synthesize executable lexical and structural detectors instead of prompting over every file. On a 100-refactoring benchmark from 61 open-source projects, it detected 56 instances at the function level against 32 for Codex CLI, and produced 40 refactorings validated by project tests and generated differential tests.

title: Software Factories, Light and Dark
blurb: Osmani distinguishes "light" software factories, where humans stay in the loop and trade concentration for speed, from "dark" ones where agents scope, build, and ship without anyone reading the code. He argues the stack is three layers deep: the loop is one agent repeating gather-act-check, the harness is the sandbox, tools, memory, and gates around it, and the factory is that loop at scale.

title: Block launches Buzz, an open source collaboration workspace on Nostr protocol for humans and AI agents to share messages, code repositories, workflows, and more (Block)
blurb: Block released Buzz, an open source collaboration workspace built on the Nostr protocol where humans and AI agents share messages, code repositories, and workflows in the same space. Building on Nostr's relay model means identity and data are keyed to user-controlled keypairs rather than a vendor account, so agents and teams can work across relays without a central server holding the workspace.

title: AI Engineering for Developers
blurb: A backend engineer with 15 years of Kubernetes and Terraform experience walks through what changes when LLMs enter production, starting from foundation model basics and ending with running agents on Google Cloud. The core argument is that the model is no longer the product, the system around it is, and that non-deterministic outputs from natural language inputs break the unit-testing assumptions most engineers rely on.

title: CommitLLM: A Fine-Tuned Pipeline for Git Commit Message Generation
blurb: CommitLLM is a three-stage pipeline that turns code diffs into Conventional Commits-compliant messages using QLoRA-fine-tuned Mistral-7B, constrained decoding, and deterministic post-processing, running on a single 16GB T4. Format compliance jumped from 22% to 98% and average message length dropped from 154.8 to 37.9 characters, with the authors noting the post-processing layers contributed more than the fine-tuning itself.

title: SynH-Rank: Quality-Aware Code Search via Diverse Data Synthesis and Hierarchical Ranking Training
blurb: This paper introduces SynH-Rank, a code search reranker that scores results on non-functional quality like execution speed, memory usage, and maintainability rather than semantic relevance alone. The authors train on LLM-synthesized data with a three-level hierarchy of high-quality relevant, low-quality relevant, and irrelevant, reporting a 20.15% gain in quality preference accuracy over backbone models and 15.80% over standard contrastive training.

title: The drivers behind software delivery inefficiency
blurb: This paper breaks down where software delivery time actually goes, attributing most inefficiency to waiting on dependencies, unclear requirements, and rework rather than slow coding. The practical reading for engineering teams is that optimizing individual developer throughput leaves the dominant bottleneck, coordination and handoff time across teams, untouched.

title: Incremental – A library for incremental computations
blurb: Jane Street's Incremental is an OCaml library for building computations that recompute only the parts affected when their inputs change, based on Umut Acar's work on self-adjusting computation. It targets spreadsheet-style calculation graphs, GUI views that fold in new data without full rebuilds, and derived data like filters or inverted mappings that must stay in sync with their source.

title: DepRepair: LLM-Based Source-Code Repair for Dependency Breaking Changes
blurb: This paper introduces DepRepair, a single-call LLM approach that patches consumer code broken by dependency updates by pulling in structured upstream evidence: filtered release notes and API diffs, a locator for affected call sites, and repair guidance keyed to the breaking-change type. On DepBench, 95 real dependency updates across four ecosystems with Docker-based test oracles, it passes 89.5% with GPT-5.5, while raw unstructured upstream evidence cuts pass rates by 7 to 23 points.

title: AI makes programming differently difficult
blurb: The author argues that AI coding assistants shifted the hard part of programming rather than removing it, trading the work of writing code for the work of reviewing, verifying, and understanding output someone else generated. The claim is that comprehension debt accumulates faster than the productivity gains show up, since a reviewer holds less context than an author and spends more effort reconstructing intent than typing the code would have cost.

title: Domain-specific harnesses
blurb: The author argues that domain specialization has moved from model weights to the harness, which controls memory, compaction, retrieval, and tools around a stateless model. The post walks through what that means in practice, including a legal workflow that persists raw file references rather than letting compaction discard them, and notes the counter-argument that frontier labs post-train models on their own harnesses.

title: IssueExec: A Test-Driven Approach for Localizing Software Engineering Issues
blurb: This paper localizes buggy code by routing through a repo's existing test suite instead of matching issue text to code directly, using domain-knowledge-enhanced test representations and hierarchical trace filtering to cut infrastructure noise. IssueExec improves function-level Recall@1 by 41.57% over the strongest baseline on SWE-bench Lite and resolves 17.72% more issues when dropped into the Agentless pipeline.

title: Ziggity – A terminal UI for Git, written in Zig
blurb: Ziggity is a terminal UI for Git written in Zig, covering line-level staging, commits, branching, interactive rebase, and history browsing with live diff previews on every panel.
