blurb: The author argues that Google Cloud's latest outage exposed how little enterprise buyers actually know about hyperscaler resilience, since published SLAs and region diagrams don't describe how failure domains really behave under stress. Contracted availability numbers say nothing about blast radius or recovery sequencing, leaving architects to design multi-region strategies against vendor claims they cannot independently verify.

title: OVH reveals semi-secret plan to fix critical Januscape hypervisor bug with mass reboots – and an Australian crash-test dummy
blurb: OVH disclosed a plan to patch a critical bug in its Januscape hypervisor through staged mass reboots across its fleet, testing the rollout on an Australian region first before touching larger European datacenters. Customers on OVH public and private cloud should expect scheduled VM restarts rather than live migration, which puts the maintenance burden on workloads that were never architected to tolerate host reboots.

title: Generosity Under Conditions: Hardening Google Cloud Access Management
blurb: Google Cloud published a guide to using IAM conditions to constrain broad roles that can't be scoped by resource binding alone, including limiting roles/iam.admin to specific role grants and gating access to AI integrations. The argument is that predefined roles plus Allow and Deny policies leave real gaps wherever a project's resources are shared across teams or workloads. For Google Cloud shops, this is a case for auditing existing admin grants rather than assuming hierarchy-level policy is sufficient.

title: Apple says it fixed a vulnerability in its Hide My Email tool that let anyone see a user's real email address; researchers first reported the issue in June 2025 (Joseph Cox/404 Media)
blurb: Apple patched a flaw in Hide My Email that exposed users' real email addresses, more than a year after researchers first reported it in June 2025. The delay is a data point for IT teams weighing Apple's privacy features as controls in enterprise identity and communication policy, where disclosure timelines matter as much as the fix itself.

title: Box adds security controls to govern AI agents working with enterprise content
blurb: Box introduced security controls that govern AI agents working with enterprise content, covering both agents built in Box and external tools connected through its MCP server, including Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini. The controls sit at the content layer rather than as a separate product, adding label-based access limits, prompt injection screening, human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions, and audit trails for compliance.

title: WekaIO revamps its AI data storage platform and unveils its first hardware for agentic workloads
blurb: WekaIO released NeuralMesh 6 alongside WEKApod Nitro, Prime, and Prime Max, its first custom storage appliances built for AI inference rather than training. The software's augmented memory grid holds KV cache in NVMe to extend GPU memory, which WekaIO benchmarks at 10x token throughput and targets buyers whose data center space and power budgets are already capped.

title: NetApp expands storage-layer AI processing with DataPelago deal
blurb: NetApp acquired DataPelago, whose Nucleus engine processes data across CPUs and GPUs at the storage layer instead of moving it to external compute clusters, for undisclosed terms. NetApp claims the technology cuts infrastructure costs by up to 80% and removes the copy step between operational systems and AI pipelines, positioning ONTAP customers against Nvidia's GPU-only cuDF framework.

title: AMD adds Microsoft to its growing roster of Helios customers as firm launches three new VMs for Azure users
blurb: Microsoft will deploy AMD's Helios rack-scale system on Azure and is launching three EPYC-powered VM families: HDv2 for large-scale data processing, HCx2 for silicon design and simulation, and ND MI455X v7 for production-scale AI inference. Azure customers standardized on Nvidia-backed instances now get an AMD path for inference and HPC, and the deal adds Microsoft to the roster of hyperscalers committing to Helios.

title: Certinia acquires AI services company Moonnox
blurb: Certinia acquired Moonnox, an AI-native automation platform for professional services, and folded its agents into Veda, Certinia's system of action. The deal adds context capture across Salesforce, Microsoft 365, G-Suite, Jira, Confluence, and Zoom, plus agents that draft proposals and statements of work, convert requirements into project blueprints, and run scope scans for delivery risk.

title: Nvidia has shipped 'hundreds of thousands of Grace standalone servers’ — GPU firm pivots messaging as CPUs take center stage in agentic data centers
blurb: Nvidia says it has shipped hundreds of thousands of standalone Grace CPU servers as agentic workloads shift GPU-to-CPU ratios toward one-to-one.

title: The EU’s AI transparency deadline is weeks away. Is your enterprise ready?
blurb: The EU AI Act's transparency obligations take effect August 2, requiring any company placing AI systems on the EU market to disclose chatbots, deepfakes, emotion recognition, and biometric categorization to users, with machine-readable markers on generated content. Non-compliance carries fines from €750K to €15M or 3% of worldwide annual revenue, and the rules reach non-EU vendors whose outputs are used in Europe.

title: UK tech trade associations hit out amidst reports DSIT could be scrapped
blurb: UK tech trade bodies including techUK and the Startup Coalition publicly opposed reported plans by incoming prime minister Andy Burnham to abolish the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology and fold tech policy into a larger business department. The reorg would put the AI Safety Institute, Sovereign AI Fund, GDS, and UKRI under new ownership, creating months of uncertainty for vendors and public-sector buyers with active UK digital transformation contracts.

title: Intel fortifies Foundry with an actual customer: Fortinet
blurb: Fortinet signed on as a customer for Intel Foundry, giving the business a named external client for its 18A process. Intel Foundry has struggled to convert marquee interest into committed volume, so a security-hardware buyer publicly committing matters more as a reference account than as revenue, and it gives enterprise buyers a second viable domestic supply option alongside TSMC's US fabs.

title: Cisco buying bargain Israeli startup for $150M+: report
blurb: Cisco is reportedly in talks to acquire Israeli exposure management vendor Zafran Security for $150M+, below the $200M valuation Zafran carried in its December round after ARR stalled near $20M. Zafran denies the deal and calls it a strategic investment, but the platform would slot into Splunk's risk analytics and backfill the gap Cisco left when it shuttered Kenna.

title: Ivanti adds predictive remediation to get ahead of the patch backlog
blurb: Ivanti introduced predictive remediation across its Autonomous Endpoint Management line, a Neurons-based capability that forecasts which patches will miss compliance deadlines and flags the blockers stalling them before service-level windows close. The pitch to IT buyers is governance rather than autonomy, with actions confined to administrator-defined guardrails, positioning Ivanti against vendors selling AI agents that patch production systems unsupervised.

title: The audit trail CIOs need before the next cyber crisis
blurb: The author argues that DORA, NIS2, and the SEC's cyber disclosure rules have shifted CIO liability from operating controls to proving, after the fact, that leadership decisions matched the risk evidence available at the time. Green dashboards that mask legacy debt, undocumented service accounts, and shadow cloud instances become evidence against you once regulators and defense counsel start reconstructing the timeline. The recommended output is a documented audit trail of how risks were escalated and decided.

title: The AI allocation trap: Record spend, vanishing returns
blurb: The author argues that the widely cited 95% AI failure rate is an allocation problem, not a technology one, and offers CIOs a portfolio framework and board scorecard to replace failure-rate tracking. Supporting data: S&P Global found the share of companies abandoning most AI initiatives rose from 17% to 42% in a year, and Gartner expects over 40% of agentic AI projects to be canceled by the end of 2027. MIT's own researchers traced the gap to integration and learning, not model quality.

title: IT leaders confident but cooked when it comes to rogue AI agents
blurb: A WanAware survey found 90% of IT and security leaders are confident they can detect a malfunctioning AI agent, but only 26% can trace the downstream impact within minutes and over 45% say it would take hours. The gap matters because agents run on standing credentials and spread damage across everything those credentials reach, which puts observability and agent identity controls on the near-term procurement list.

title: The latest Chinese AI models may indeed work for enterprises, but only in a handful of specific applications
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen3.8 Max and Moonshot's Kimi K3 are cheap and fast enough that enterprises are reconsidering Chinese models, but the analysts quoted here scope them to coding, multilingual processing, high-volume document analysis, and synthetic-data generation. The argument is to treat jurisdiction, provenance, and licensing as ordinary supply-chain diligence rather than a blanket ban, while keeping the models away from regulated data and un-supervised customer-facing work where a hallucination creates legal exposure.

title: WP2Shell: Pre-Authentication RCE in WordPress Core
blurb: Searchlight Cyber disclosed a pre-authentication RCE in WordPress Core affecting versions 6.9.0 through 6.9.4 and 7.0.0 through 7.0.1, exploitable by anonymous users against a stock install with no plugins. Patching to 7.0.2 or 6.9.5 is the only durable fix, with WAF blocks on the batch REST API as a temporary stopgap that may break legitimate traffic.

title: Critical NGINX Vulnerability Can Crash Workers and May Allow Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched CVE-2026-42533, a heap buffer overflow in nginx's script engine that a remote unauthenticated attacker can trigger with crafted HTTP requests, crashing workers and potentially allowing remote code execution. Fixes landed in nginx 1.30.4, 1.31.3, and NGINX Plus 37.0.3.1, but exposure depends on config: only a regex-based map referenced after an earlier capture is affected, and F5 has not yet listed fixed builds for Ingress Controller, Gateway Fabric, App Protect WAF, or Instance Manager.

title: Why goodput matters more than throughput for LLM serving
blurb: The author argues that throughput is the wrong headline metric for LLM serving, since requests per second can climb while TTFT and TPOT quietly blow past targets. Goodput counts only completed requests that meet those latency SLOs, making it the number worth tuning against. The walkthrough serves Qwen2.5-7B on vLLM on a single A10G in EKS, driving load with GuideLLM and instrumenting through Prometheus, Grafana, and NVIDIA's DCGM exporter, with the Kubernetes manifests published for reuse against your own traffic.

title: Now in preview: Find and fix software vulnerabilities with CodeMender
blurb: Google put CodeMender, its managed code security agent from DeepMind, into preview, offering automated vulnerability scanning and patching through the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform or as part of AI Threat Defense. It prioritizes fixes by running proof-of-concept exploits to confirm exploitability, and lets teams swap models to trade off cost against scan depth, with third-party frontier models due later this year.

title: Airbus takes flight from AWS. What happens next is critical
blurb:
Airbus is moving workloads off AWS, and the author argues the execution matters more than the decision itself. Repatriation at that scale means rebuilding managed-service dependencies, identity, and networking on infrastructure the team now runs itself, and the cost case only holds if utilization stays high enough to beat what the hyperscaler was charging.

Note: the supplied context is The Register's "most popular" sidebar, not the article body — no details on Airbus's target platform, timeline, or scale. Blurb is written from the headline only. Pull the article text if you want specifics in it.

title: Supercharging pgvector: 4x faster HNSW vector search with AlloyDB
blurb: Google released columnar engine accelerated HNSW for AlloyDB in preview, delivering up to 4x higher QPS on pgvector similarity search than standard PostgreSQL HNSW at comparable recall. The speedup comes from AlloyDB's in-memory columnar cache handling the index scan, so teams running RAG workloads can cut vector-search fleet size instead of sharding out to a dedicated vector database.

title: New Bit2Watt Attack Could Let Cloud Tenants Disrupt Power Grids Without an Exploit
blurb: Three Zhejiang University researchers described Bit2Watt, a technique that lets an ordinary cloud tenant oscillate a data center's power draw at 1.2 to 6 kHz using only their own GPU workloads, with no exploit or privilege escalation. The harder variant hides the modulation inside a real LLM training run by tweaking hyperparameters, making it blend into normal training noise rather than fingerprintable kernel behavior.

title: Isolation Failure From Shared Storage: Characterizing and Exploiting Page-Cache SCA Leakage Across Containers and VMs
blurb: Researchers demonstrated that the host page cache leaks file-access timing across container and VM boundaries, breaking isolation on Docker, gVisor, Kata with QEMU or Cloud Hypervisor, and QEMU/KVM whenever the I/O path touches shared host-cacheable files through OverlayFS, virtio-fs, or loop devices. Direct I/O and dedicated block devices largely kill the signal, which the authors showed recovering coarse activity from a WordPress and MySQL deployment.

title: FailureAtlas: A Taxonomy of Failure Modes in Multi-Provider LLM Serving Infrastructure
blurb: This paper introduces FailureAtlas, a two-axis taxonomy of failure modes in multi-provider LLM gateways, classifying them by origin layer (network, streaming, session state, model behavior, cost governance) and by whether they fail loud or silent. The authors' main finding is that the worst failures are silent: they return HTTP 200, pass every health check, and corrupt application state. Five cataloged entries include root-cause analysis, and three ship with reproduction scripts.

title: Scalding hot AI accelerators have put datacenters in hot water. Two-phase cooling could chill them out
blurb: Startup Accelsius claims its two-phase refrigerant cooling drops GPU temperatures by up to 14°C, demonstrated by converting a liquid-cooled Dell PowerEdge to run on refrigerants instead of water. The pitch for datacenter operators is headroom as accelerator power draw climbs past what single-phase liquid loops handle, along with removing water from the rack and the leak risk that comes with it.

title: Why your agent needs access to your documentation
blurb: After shipping an in-product agent with roughly 30 native tools, the authors found that a single `search_knowledge_base` tool was the most-used one across 1,192 conversations, nearly matching all native tools combined. Documentation search acted as the failover in 32.1% of conversations, answering setup and troubleshooting questions no purpose-built tool covered. For CNCF projects with large, fast-moving docs, the split is native tools for current state, docs for what it means.

title: The drivers behind software delivery inefficiency
blurb: Researchers surveyed engineering organizations to identify what actually slows software delivery, finding that most lost time comes from waiting on other teams, unclear requirements, and flaky or slow build and test pipelines rather than from individual coding speed. The authors argue that these delays compound, since each handoff adds queue time that no amount of local developer-productivity tooling recovers. For platform teams, the leverage is in CI reliability and reducing cross-team dependencies, not in per-developer metrics.

title: Shipyard: How We Built Slack's Next-Generation EC2 Platform
blurb: Slack's infrastructure team detailed Shipyard, the EC2 platform that replaced continuous Chef convergence on long-lived instances with immutable, deployable artifacts and progressive rollouts. The team hit the limits of configuration management across tens of thousands of instances, where drift was inevitable and service-level deploys required coordinating changes across multiple layers. Shipyard pushes that responsibility into build pipelines and automated safety checks, giving EC2 workloads that could not containerize the same rollout guarantees as app delivery.

title: Google Cloud outage shows it’s still hard to understand hyperscalers’ real resilience regimes
blurb: The author argues that Google Cloud's latest outage exposed how little customers can actually verify about hyperscaler resilience, since published region and zone isolation guarantees rarely map to the shared control planes that fail in practice. Multi-region architectures priced as insurance still inherit correlated failure paths that no SLA document describes. The practical takeaway is to treat provider resilience claims as unverified until an incident retro proves the blast radius.

title: Generosity Under Conditions: Hardening Google Cloud Access Management
blurb: A Google Cloud developer advocate walks through using IAM conditions to constrain roles that resource-level bindings cannot, including capping `roles/iam.admin` so holders cannot grant themselves arbitrary roles or mint new ones. The post pairs that with a conditional-access example for AI integrations, and argues conditions are the practical fallback when least privilege breaks down on shared projects where multiple teams and workloads touch the same resources.

title: Platform engineering for the agentic enterprise: Managing applications, resources, and AI agents
blurb: The author argues that internal developer platforms were built on the assumption that a human developer is the primary consumer, and that AI agents provisioning infrastructure, deploying apps, and investigating incidents break that model. The proposed fix is a single governance layer covering both, with humans and agents hitting interfaces suited to their capabilities under the same policy enforcement, RBAC, and audit trail rather than a bolted-on API surface for agents.

title: AI speeds software development. Is your secret security keeping up?
blurb: HashiCorp argues that AI-assisted development is outpacing secret management, citing GitGuardian data showing nearly 30M secrets detected in public GitHub repos in 2025, up 34% year over year, with AI-generated code leaking at roughly twice the GitHub-wide rate. The post pushes detection left into IDE scanning, pre-commit hooks, and CI/CD checks rather than post-commit cleanup. It also argues remediation needs exposure context, since undifferentiated findings turn into a backlog no security team can prioritize.

title: ClouDens: Operational Context-Aware Anomaly Detection for Large-scale Cloud System Monitoring
blurb: The authors studied telemetry logs from IBM Cloud Console and propose ClouDens, an anomaly detection framework that partitions high-dimensional telemetry into domain-guided subsets and models service dependencies as a context-aware graph fed to spatio-temporal GNNs. It beats a GRU baseline on NAB scores for count-based features, catching anomalies earlier and with wider coverage. The paper also quantifies how much feature subsetting, scoring strategy, and sparsity imputation each move detection performance, which matters for anyone benchmarking their own alerting stack.

title: The top content formats & types that earn AI search citations
blurb: HubSpot's 2026 State of AEO Report found 58% of marketers are now optimizing content for answer engines, and the pages that actually earn citations share structural traits rather than topical ones. Question-led headings, self-contained direct-answer passages, schema markup, and internal linking that maps entity relationships are what let AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity extract and attribute a passage. The practical shift is writing sections that stand alone out of context, since extraction happens at the passage level, not the page level.

title: Advertise in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI opened ChatGPT advertising to all buyers through a self-serve Ads Manager, with campaigns targeting users mid-research as they compare options and weigh tradeoffs. Ads are labeled and kept separate from ChatGPT responses, and early testers include Best Buy, which is treating the placement as a way to reach shoppers before they reach a retailer or search result.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking has replaced functional depth as the skill she screens for, and that AI fluency is now expected at every level rather than treated as a senior-track differentiator. Her more immediate problem is triage: AI-generated output has multiplied faster than the review capacity to judge it, so the constraint has shifted from producing work to deciding which work is worth keeping.

title: You're doing "Activation" wrong
blurb: The author argues the standard activation rate, users hitting an aha milestone divided by signups, has stopped working as a growth metric because AI-driven acquisition brings in more users but not the right ones. Activation is a company problem rather than a growth-team problem, and teams that optimize the ratio in isolation end up trading long-term efficiency for short-term wins. The piece draws on the author's work as CPGO at Fyxer, where curating strategy and ICP documentation for AI tooling still failed to make the real problem areas obvious.

title: Durov Says Telegram Will Ship Native Gram Wallet to a Billion Users
blurb: Pavel Durov said Telegram will embed a native, non-custodial Gram wallet in every version of the app this summer, supporting fee-free transfers to a user base above one billion. The rollout follows Telegram replacing the TON Foundation as the network's primary operator and cutting fees roughly sixfold, though Durov has not given a launch date, a supported-asset list, or the fate of the existing custodial Wallet.

title: Aztec upgrades to V5 in alpha, adding full private execution environment to decentralized Ethereum L2
blurb: Aztec released V5 in alpha, adding a full private execution environment to its decentralized Ethereum L2. The release centers on client-side proving, which lets phones and laptops generate the computation-heavy ZK proofs that previously required dedicated hardware or a trusted remote prover.

title: Telegram plans ‘largest rollout of a non-custodial crypto wallet in human history’
blurb: Telegram said it will roll out a non-custodial TON wallet to its full user base, which it describes as the largest such deployment to date. The messaging app has roughly 1B monthly active users, so even single-digit activation rates would put self-custody wallets in front of an audience larger than every centralized exchange's combined user count.

title: Russia passes landmark crypto bill allowing regulated retail trading
blurb: Russia's State Duma passed a bill legalizing regulated retail crypto trading, capping non-qualified investors at 300,000 rubles in annual purchases while leaving qualified investors unrestricted. The framework moves Russian retail flow out of gray-market OTC desks and onto licensed venues, giving domestic exchanges a compliance path that has been absent since the 2020 digital assets law.

title: Movement Labs Files for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy
blurb: MVMT Labs, the developer behind the Move-based Ethereum L2 Movement, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in Delaware on July 15, listing under $1M in assets against $1M to $10M in liabilities and up to 999 creditors. Largest creditors include ousted co-founder Rushi Manche and custodian Anchorage Digital, closing out a collapse that began with the December 2024 MOVE launch and the market-making deal that dumped 66M tokens on day two.

title: Arcus rolls out 24/7 tokenized US stocks and perpetual markets on Robinhood Chain
blurb: Arcus launched 24/7 trading for more than 95 Stock Tokens on Robinhood Chain, extending tokenized US equity exposure beyond standard market hours. The rollout also includes beta perpetual markets collateralized in USDG, putting spot equity tokens and leveraged derivatives on the same chain and settling both against a single stablecoin margin base.

title: Hackers steal $23.7 million in crypto from Ostium in off-chain attack
blurb: Ostium lost $23.75M from its liquidity provider vault after an attacker compromised the off-chain infrastructure feeding prices into the Arbitrum-based perps protocol, submitting fake price reports and opening and closing large positions for artificial profit. Trader collateral sat in a separate contract and was untouched, but trading has been paused since July 16, and the exploiter swapped the stolen USDC for 12,080 ETH and routed 10,540 of it through Tornado Cash.

title: Bitcoin Rallies After Bessent Says Clarity Act at ‘1-Yard Line’
blurb: Bitcoin rallied after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the CLARITY Act is at the "1-yard line," signaling that US market-structure legislation for digital assets could clear Congress within weeks. The bill would split oversight between the SEC and CFTC and define when a token stops being a security, ending the case-by-case enforcement regime that has pushed protocol teams offshore since 2023.

title: Aztec Launches Alpha V5 on Mainnet With Faster Private Proving
blurb: Aztec Network released Alpha V5 on mainnet, which the team says proves a fully private transaction in about 2.5 seconds on a consumer laptop and cuts fees for a private token transfer to under $0.05. Both figures are self-reported and roughly double the speed and halve the cost of V4, with client-side proving keeping transaction data on the user's device. Nyx, a wallet offering private transfers and private Aave yield, is the first app live.

title: White House pushes Senate Democrats to take 'historic' crypto Clarity Act ethics deal
blurb: The White House is pressing Senate Democrats to accept an undisclosed conflict-of-interest agreement that Trump negotiated with Republicans, the last unresolved section of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act. Democratic negotiators including Kirsten Gillibrand, Ruben Gallego, and Angela Alsobrooks say they have not been briefed on the terms, leaving the final draft of the market structure bill stalled for several days.

title: Augustus raises $180 million to build a clearing bank for the AI and stablecoin era
blurb: Augustus raised $180M at a $1B valuation led by Tiger Global to build a federally chartered clearing bank that settles across traditional payment rails and blockchain networks. The company holds conditional OCC approval for a US national bank charter and already clears billions of euros annually through its Finnish entity, with plans to add dollar clearing and customers in Latin America, Southeast Asia, the Middle East and Africa.

title: Trump-backed crypto ethics rule puts DOJ in charge of enforcement, prohibits federal officials from issuing cryptocurrencies
blurb: President Trump signed newly approved ethics language that bars federal officials from issuing cryptocurrencies and hands enforcement authority to the Department of Justice. The rule draws a line around token issuance by government personnel while leaving holding and trading rules largely untouched, and DOJ oversight shifts crypto ethics violations from an internal compliance matter to a prosecutable one.

title: Grayscale Files S-1 for Spot Worldcoin ETF
blurb: Grayscale filed an S-1 with the SEC on July 20 to launch a spot Worldcoin ETF holding WLD, the token behind the World Network iris-scanning identity system. The filing lists no fee, ticker, or listing venue yet, and extends Grayscale's single-asset altcoin push alongside earlier Solana and Zcash registrations. WLD carries a circulating market cap of about $1.35B.

title: S&P and Pantera Launch Revenue-Screened Digital Asset Index
blurb: S&P Dow Jones Indices and Pantera Capital launched the S&P Pantera Digital Asset Index, an 18-constituent benchmark that screens for consecutive quarters of positive protocol revenue verified by Artemis onchain data, excluding meme coins and Bitcoin. Constituents including Hyperliquid, Solana, and Aave generated over $3B of annualized revenue in the trailing two quarters, and Pantera says it is in talks with asset managers about ETFs tracking the index.

title: Morpho Launches Fixed-Rate Lending Protocol Midnight on Base
blurb: Morpho launched Midnight, a fixed-rate, fixed-term credit protocol, on Base, alongside a Markets App that lets users lend and borrow directly from those markets. Most DeFi lending prices on variable rates that float with utilization, so locking a rate for a defined term moves Morpho closer to traditional fixed-income credit and targets fintechs, institutions, and credit desks. Base is the only supported network at launch.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb:
Visa announced a new stablecoin platform, extending a crypto push that already includes USDC settlement pilots on Solana and Ethereum. The launch positions Visa as infrastructure for bank-issued stablecoins rather than a competitor to them, giving regulated issuers distribution across Visa's existing merchant network.

(46 words. Flagging: the Context field was empty, so I wrote from the headline plus Visa's documented USDC settlement history. If the Bloomberg piece has specifics — launch partners, chains, volume figures — those belong in sentence two instead of my framing.)

title: Robinhood Prediction Market Revenue to Top Crypto, Analysts Say
blurb: Analysts at Bernstein and Piper Sandler expect Robinhood's prediction market revenue to overtake its crypto trading revenue as soon as Q2. The shift marks event contracts moving from a retail novelty to a core exchange revenue line, and it puts pressure on crypto venues that have treated spot and perps trading volume as their primary fee base.

title: Crypto Clarity Act still at mercy of ethics section as Democrats balk at Trump deal
blurb: Senate Democrats and the White House remain split over who enforces the conflict-of-interest limits in the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, with Democrats pushing for state attorneys general and Republicans insisting on the US attorney general. Trump has agreed to restrictions covering himself, the vice president, and members of Congress, and the rest of the market structure bill is expected to move once the ethics section is settled before the August 7 recess deadline.

title: Russia’s parliament passes crypto market law with $3,800 annual cap for retail investors
blurb: Russia's State Duma passed the country's first comprehensive crypto law, creating a registry-based licensing regime for exchanges and depositories that takes effect Sept. 1, with retail purchases capped at roughly $3,800 per year per licensed intermediary. The law keeps the ban on domestic crypto payments but permits digital currencies in foreign trade settlements, giving Russian firms a sanctioned-adjacent channel as the EU tightens crypto restrictions.

title: Chiral Analysis of Smart Contracts: Detecting Vulnerabilities from Relational Inconsistencies Across Business Paths
blurb: This paper introduces chiral analysis, a static method that treats paired business paths in a Solidity contract (single vs. batch entry points, quote vs. execution, buy vs. sell) as implicit specifications for each other and flags divergence in guards, state transitions, value flows, or failure behavior. Their ChiralDetector prototype narrowed 3,217 ranked path pairs on the Phi protocol to 13 unique issues, including Merkle proof reuse across arts, fee unit mismatches, and refund propagation gaps.

title: The Digital Chamber sues Illinois over incoming crypto transaction tax
blurb: The Digital Chamber sued Illinois over a newly passed law imposing a 0.2% tax on digital asset transactions, arguing the state overstepped its authority in taxing on-chain activity. A per-transaction levy hits high-frequency trading and DeFi routing hardest, and a win for Illinois would give other states a template for taxing transfers rather than realized gains.

title: Canton developer Digital Asset raises additional $10 million at same $2 billion equity valuation
blurb: Digital Asset, the developer behind the Canton Network, raised an additional $10M from Shinhan Financial Group and SC Ventures at the same $2B equity valuation, expanding its round to $365M. Holding the valuation flat while adding strategic banking investors signals the raise is about distribution into regulated institutional markets rather than fresh price discovery.

title: Jack Mallers steps down as XXI Capital CEO as Tether's plans to merge three bitcoin firms falls
blurb: Twenty One Capital named Raphael Zagury CEO, replacing Jack Mallers, and abandoned Tether's proposed three-way merger with Strike and Elektron Energy. Mallers returns to Strike, which will stay independent, while XXI weighs a two-way combination with Elektron and pivots toward acquiring operating businesses and building bitcoin-backed lending. XXI was little changed in pre-market trading.

title: Augustus raises $180 million Series B at $1 billion valuation to build the ‘Global Dollar Bank’
blurb: Augustus raised $180M in a Series B at a $1B valuation to build what it calls a "Global Dollar Bank," a settlement layer that routes payments across stablecoins and traditional rails including Swift, ACH, and SEPA. The pitch targets treasury and cross-border flows where firms want stablecoin settlement speed without abandoning the bank infrastructure their counterparties still use.

title: Satsuma shareholders approve bitcoin treasury liquidation and London delisting
blurb: Satsuma Technology shareholders voted to liquidate the company's bitcoin treasury and delist from the London Stock Exchange, less than a year after raising $218 million to fund the strategy. The reversal marks one of the fastest unwinds among the public companies that added bitcoin to their balance sheets, and removes a UK-listed vehicle for equity investors seeking indirect bitcoin exposure.

title: Congress weighs its role as fight over sports betting prediction market oversight forges on
blurb: Congress is weighing federal legislation to settle whether the CFTC or state gaming regulators oversee sports-related prediction markets, as the jurisdictional fight between Kalshi, tribal groups, and state agencies continues in the courts. A statutory answer would remove the regulatory ambiguity that has kept event-contract volume concentrated in a handful of venues operating under CFTC designation while facing state cease-and-desist orders.

title: Ousted founder’s $1.6 million claim tops Movement Labs’ bankruptcy filing
blurb: Ousted Movement Labs co-founder Rushi Manche filed a $1.6M claim against the company, the largest single creditor claim in its bankruptcy filing. Movement, which built the Move-based MVMT chain, reported assets of $100,001 to $500,000 against liabilities of up to $10M, leaving most claimants unlikely to recover in full.

title: Galaxy launches Bitcoin quantum computing initiative, commits up to $5 million in developer grants
blurb: Galaxy launched the Bitcoin Quantum Readiness Initiative, committing up to $5M in developer grants for work on post-quantum signature schemes and migration paths for Bitcoin's ECDSA-secured UTXOs. Roughly 25% of circulating BTC sits in addresses with exposed public keys, making them the first at risk if cryptographically relevant quantum hardware arrives.

title: Solana, Hyperliquid ETFs dominate altcoin fund flows despite flying under the radar
blurb: Solana ETFs have accumulated $904M in assets under management and Hyperliquid funds have pulled in $350M in net inflows, making them the largest altcoin ETF categories outside of bitcoin and ether products. The flows arrived with little attention relative to the launch coverage each product received, suggesting steady allocator demand rather than retail-driven speculation in the two categories.

title: Bitget plans US expansion ‘with or without’ Clarity Act, CEO says
blurb: Bitget will proceed with its US expansion regardless of whether the Clarity Act passes, CEO Gracy Chen said, reversing the exchange's 2022 decision to shelve those plans. The move puts Bitget on a path to compete with Coinbase and Kraken for US order flow without waiting for federal market-structure rules that would settle which tokens fall under SEC versus CFTC jurisdiction.

title: Uphold launches fractional share trading in US for more than 4,000 stocks and ETFs
blurb: Uphold launched fractional share trading in the US, giving customers access to more than 4,000 stocks and ETFs alongside its existing crypto order book. The company is positioning single-step asset swaps as the differentiator, letting users sell bitcoin and buy equities in one transaction rather than routing through a fiat settlement leg, which puts it against Robinhood and Revolut in the combined crypto-equities brokerage tier.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro took a strategic stake in Extended, a Starknet-based perpetual futures decentralized exchange, as part of a $12.5M round reported by CoinDesk, and plans to pair the venue with Zengo, the self-custody wallet it acquired in April for roughly $70M. The tie-up would let eToro offer onchain perpetuals across Extended's 100-plus crypto, equities, FX, and commodities markets to wallet users who retain custody of their assets.

title: Coinbase says ‘low-risk’ config change behind 50-minute July 14 outage impacting trading, card transactions
blurb: Coinbase attributed its 50-minute July 14 outage, which halted trading and card transactions, to a configuration change its internal review had classified as low-risk. The exchange said user funds were never at risk and that transactions stuck in-flight during the disruption settled once service was restored.

title: Malaysia shuts down Balaji Srinivasan’s Network School in Forest City
blurb: Malaysia revoked the operating license for Network School, the digital nomad commune founded by Balaji Srinivasan in Forest City, and ordered it to cease operations on July 22. The school had run three-month cohorts since 2024 as a physical testbed for Srinivasan's network state thesis, drawing founders and remote workers to the largely vacant Johor development.

title: London Stock Exchange’s LSE 24 signals a bet on digital, AI-driven markets: TD Cowen
blurb: The London Stock Exchange will launch LSE 24, a 24/5 venue trading 17:00 to 07:50, with testing slated for end-2026 and ETPs listed first in H1 2027. TD Cowen reads the move as a bet that digital, AI-driven market structure is where incumbent exchanges have to compete, putting traditional venues on the same always-on footing crypto markets already run.

title: Jack Mallers leaves Twenty One as Strike exits Tether’s three-way bitcoin merger
blurb: Twenty One Capital named Raphael Zagury CEO, replacing founder Jack Mallers, after Strike withdrew from the Tether-backed three-way merger that would have combined the three companies. The exit leaves Twenty One's bitcoin treasury vehicle without the payments business it was structured around, and under a CEO drawn from Zagury's investment background rather than Mallers' consumer-facing bitcoin operation.

title: Jack Mallers Steps Down as CEO of Twenty One Capital
blurb: Jack Mallers stepped down as CEO of Twenty One Capital, the Tether-backed bitcoin treasury company he founded, to return full-time to Strike, his bitcoin payments firm. Raphael Zagury takes over as CEO of the company, which trades under $XXI and was assembled as one of the largest bitcoin treasury vehicles positioned against Michael Saylor's Strategy.

title: Saylor Opposes Bitcoin's BIP-110 in 110-Point Essay
blurb: Michael Saylor published a 110-point essay on X opposing BIP-110, the one-year anti-spam soft fork that would bundle seven restrictions on data-heavy Bitcoin transactions, arguing consensus rules cannot judge the intent behind valid fee-paying transactions. Signaling blocks sit at 0.86% of the current difficulty period against the 55% needed for early lock-in, ahead of a mandatory signaling window opening around August 7.

title: Bank of Korea prepares for live CBDC transactions with 9 banks in September
blurb: The Bank of Korea will begin phase two of its CBDC pilot in September, expanding real-transaction testing to nine banks including KB Kookmin, Shinhan, Hana, and Woori. The BOK will supply the institutional infrastructure while each bank issues and manages its own deposit tokens, a structure aimed at commercialization rather than another closed trial.

title: Galaxy sets up $5 million fund to help shield Bitcoin against quantum computing threats
blurb: Galaxy Digital committed $5M to a Bitcoin Quantum Readiness Initiative, funding developers building quantum-resistant signature schemes, wallet migration tooling, and security audits. Roughly 6.9M BTC, about $461B at current prices, sits in address types that would be exposed if quantum hardware matures, and any post-quantum migration would require coordination across developers, wallets, and exchanges on a scale well beyond Taproot.

title: Bernstein Lifts Robinhood Target to $160 on Prediction Markets
blurb: Bernstein raised its Robinhood price target to $160 from $130, projecting prediction-market revenue will grow at a 64% CAGR to roughly $1.7B by 2028 and overtake crypto trading revenue as soon as Q2 2026. The call rests on Rothera, the CFTC-licensed exchange Robinhood built from LedgerX with Susquehanna, which has processed over 3.5B contracts but still handles only about 16% of Robinhood's event-contract volume.

title: Live markets: Bitcoin pulls back from five-week high on new Trump threats over Iran
blurb: Bitcoin pulled back from a five-week high after fresh Trump threats over Iran, even as spot bitcoin ETFs logged a fifth consecutive day of inflows, the longest streak since April. Separately, U.K. treasury company Satsuma's shareholders voted to sell its remaining 668 BTC and wind down, joining Bitcoin Standard and Empery Digital in abandoning digital asset treasury strategies.

title: UK Parliament begins inquiry into banking chokepoint for crypto businesses
blurb: The UK's Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group opened a cross-party inquiry into whether British banks have systematically denied crypto firms accounts and blocked crypto-related payments. The group is taking written evidence from the banking, payments, fintech, and crypto sectors over six weeks before publishing findings and recommendations to the government.

title: Coinbase shifts Base away from social experiments toward financial infrastructure
blurb: Jesse Pollak handed leadership of the Base app team to Jordan Fish, better known as Cobie, after conceding that Base's bets on social and creator features, including content coins, largely failed. Pollak will refocus on Base chain infrastructure around trading, stablecoin payments, and AI agents, following Farcaster's sale, Zora's pivot to Solana, and last week's launch of Robinhood's competing Ethereum L2.

title: Ondo Enables Tokenized Stock Collateral on OndoPerps
blurb: Ondo Finance enabled its tokenized equity tokens SPYon and QQQon as margin collateral on OndoPerps, letting traders back perpetual futures positions without converting to stablecoins first. An initial $100,000 per-asset notional cap applies, and the venue is blocked to US persons since neither the perps nor the Ondo Stocks tokens are registered under US securities law.

title: Bitcoin Rallies After Bessent Says Clarity Act at ‘1-Yard Line’
blurb: Bitcoin rallied after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the Clarity Act, which would split digital-asset oversight between the SEC and CFTC, is at the "1-yard line" in Congress. Passage would give exchanges, custodians, and stablecoin issuers a statutory test for which tokens are securities, ending the enforcement-driven regime that has kept most banks and payment firms on the sidelines.

title: Arcus rolls out 24/7 tokenized US stocks and perpetual markets on Robinhood Chain
blurb: Arcus launched 24/7 trading for more than 95 tokenized US stocks on Robinhood Chain, alongside a beta perpetual futures market collateralized in USDG. Continuous equity trading outside NYSE hours moves settlement and price discovery onto chain rather than T+1 clearing, which puts tokenized-equity venues in direct competition with brokerages for after-hours order flow.

title: Russia passes landmark crypto bill allowing regulated retail trading
blurb: Russia's State Duma passed a crypto bill legalizing regulated retail trading, capping non-qualified investors at 300,000 rubles in annual purchases. The framework moves Russian crypto activity from a gray zone onto licensed venues with KYC obligations, giving domestic exchanges a compliance path while the purchase cap keeps retail volume small enough that most flow stays offshore.

title: Telegram plans ‘largest rollout of a non-custodial crypto wallet in human history’
blurb: Telegram said it will bundle a non-custodial crypto wallet into its messaging app for a user base approaching 1B, which it called the largest such rollout ever attempted. Putting self-custody and stablecoin transfers one tap from a chat window turns a messaging app into a payments rail, and it does so outside the KYC and sponsor-bank structure that regulated fintechs operate under.

title: Revolut lands full Australian banking licence, the first global fintech to do so, marking its first banking entity in APAC
blurb: Revolut secured a full, unrestricted ADI licence from APRA, the first global fintech to win one in Australia and its first banking entity in APAC. The company will invest roughly AU$400M over five years to migrate 1.2 million Australian customers onto deposit accounts paying 3%, taking direct aim at the Big Four with a fourth banking licence after the UK, EEA, and Mexico.

title: Augustus raises $180 million to build a clearing bank for the AI and stablecoin era
blurb: Augustus raised $180M at a $1B valuation, led by Tiger Global, to build an AI-native clearing bank for stablecoin-era payments rather than issuing its own stablecoin. The company holds conditional OCC approval for a national bank charter and already clears euros through a regulated Finnish entity, targeting the correspondent-banking layer where settlement still takes two days and closes on weekends.

title: White House pushes Senate Democrats to take 'historic' crypto Clarity Act ethics deal
blurb: The White House is pressing Senate Democrats to accept an undisclosed conflict-of-interest agreement that Trump struck with Republican negotiators, the last unresolved section of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act. The provision would restrict senior officials' personal crypto business ties, but Democratic negotiators including Kirsten Gillibrand and Ruben Gallego say they have not been briefed on its terms, leaving the market structure bill's final draft stalled.

title: Goldman Sachs creates private markets platform as rich investors seek the next SpaceX and Stripe
blurb: Goldman Sachs launched an alternative investments platform for wealthy clients and family offices, combining its existing alternatives business with new teams focused on direct stakes in late-stage private companies and secondary trading of those positions. The move follows two decades of arranging pre-IPO access to names like SpaceX, Stripe, and Canva, and targets clients steering capital toward AI infrastructure such as data centers.

title: Trump-backed crypto ethics rule puts DOJ in charge of enforcement, prohibits federal officials from issuing cryptocurrencies
blurb: Newly approved federal ethics language signed by President Trump prohibits federal officials from issuing cryptocurrencies and hands enforcement to the DOJ rather than an independent ethics body. Putting the DOJ in charge means enforcement discretion sits with an executive-branch agency, and the rule covers token issuance rather than holdings, leaving officials' existing crypto positions and affiliated ventures largely untouched.

title: $100 Million Bet on Venezuelan Fintech Cashea Draws Global Investors
blurb: Cashea, Venezuela's largest fintech, raised $100M across two financing rounds from global investors betting on consumer lending in a market where bank credit has been effectively frozen for years. The buy-now-pay-later company has scaled by underwriting installment purchases in an economy with almost no functioning credit bureau, making it a test case for whether fintech lenders can build risk models from transaction data alone.

title: S&P and Pantera Launch Revenue-Screened Digital Asset Index
blurb: S&P Dow Jones Indices and Pantera Capital launched the S&P Pantera Digital Asset Index, an 18-constituent benchmark that screens tokens for verified protocol revenue and excludes Bitcoin and meme coins. Constituents including Hyperliquid, Solana, and Aave generated over $3B in annualized revenue over the trailing two quarters, with eligibility modeled on the S&P 500's earnings criterion and verified against Artemis onchain data. No tracking product has launched yet.

title: Robinhood Prediction Market Revenue to Top Crypto, Analysts Say
blurb: Analysts at Bernstein and Piper Sandler said Robinhood's prediction-market revenue is set to surpass its crypto trading revenue as soon as Q2. Event contracts carry event-based fees that are less exposed to crypto volume swings, giving Robinhood a steadier revenue line while CFTC oversight of prediction markets remains unsettled.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb: Visa is building a new stablecoin platform that lets banks and fintechs issue and settle their own tokens on top of Visa's existing network, extending a crypto push that already moves USDC across four blockchains. The move puts Visa in direct competition with the stablecoin infrastructure providers its issuer clients would otherwise use, and positions the card network as a settlement layer rather than a bystander to it.

title: Analyzing the evidence that helps businesses win “product not received” disputes
blurb: Stripe analyzed evidence packets from one million disputes over 16 weeks and found that "product not received" claims submitted with delivery confirmation, a GPS carrier scan map, and a recipient signature won at a 44 percentage point higher rate than those without. Delivery confirmation alone accounted for 27 points of that lift, and timing mattered: evidence submitted after delivery was confirmed beat evidence showing only that a package had shipped. The gap persists because shipping data and dispute workflows sit in separate systems, making order-to-dispute matching manual.

title: Apple to Launch ‘Upgrade’ Device Leasing Program With Klarna to Spur Sales
blurb: Apple is preparing to launch Apple Upgrade, a device leasing program built with Klarna that would let customers finance and roll over hardware on a subscription basis. The move puts a BNPL lender inside Apple's core hardware sales motion, shifting device revenue toward recurring payments and handing Klarna underwriting volume at a scale few consumer-credit partnerships reach.

title: Five tech giants are hiding $1.65tn in AI debt, using the trick that toppled Enron
blurb: A Nikkei study found Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle carry $1.65T in off-balance-sheet debt tied to AI data centers, up roughly eightfold in four years and exceeding the $1.35T they report outright. The structures are legal joint ventures rather than Enron-style concealment, but leases roll onto the books once facilities go live, and S&P has already cut Oracle's rating over stretched leverage.

title: Crypto Clarity Act still at mercy of ethics section as Democrats balk at Trump deal
blurb: The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act remains stalled over its ethics section, with Senate Democrats and the White House deadlocked on whether state attorneys general or the US attorney general enforces a crypto-holdings ban covering the president, vice president, and members of Congress. August 7, the last day before Senate recess, is the effective deadline, and the rest of the market-structure bill is expected to move once that single provision is settled.

title: Augustus raises $180 million Series B at $1 billion valuation to build the ‘Global Dollar Bank’
blurb: Augustus raised $180M in a Series B at a $1B valuation to build what it calls a "Global Dollar Bank," routing payments across stablecoins alongside Swift, ACH, and SEPA. The pitch is a single account that settles domestically on traditional rails and cross-border on stablecoins, targeting the treasury teams that currently stitch those flows together across multiple banking partners.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro became a strategic investor in Extended, a Starknet-based onchain perpetuals exchange, in a round CoinDesk pegged at $12.5M, and paired it with Zengo, the self-custody wallet eToro bought in April for roughly $70M. Combining Zengo's custody with Extended's cross-asset derivatives engine lets eToro offer onchain perps to users who keep control of their assets, mirroring Robinhood's push into onchain product suites.

title: Congress weighs its role as fight over sports betting prediction market oversight forges on
blurb: Congress is weighing federal legislation to settle whether the CFTC or state gaming regulators oversee sports-related prediction markets, as Kalshi and Robinhood keep offering event contracts under CFTC registration while states issue cease-and-desist orders. A federal preemption ruling would hand a single license the reach that sportsbooks currently assemble state by state, reshaping payments, KYC, and settlement stacks for the entire category.

title: Uphold launches fractional share trading in US for more than 4,000 stocks and ETFs
blurb: Uphold launched fractional share trading in the US, giving customers access to more than 4,000 stocks and ETFs alongside the crypto and metals already on the platform. The pitch is single-step conversion, selling Bitcoin to buy Berkshire Hathaway shares without an intermediate cash leg, which puts Uphold against Robinhood and eToro on multi-asset brokerage rather than crypto alone.

title: Coinbase says ‘low-risk’ config change behind 50-minute July 14 outage impacting trading, card transactions
blurb: Coinbase attributed its 50-minute July 14 outage, which halted trading and card transactions, to a configuration change its internal review had classified as low-risk. User funds were never at risk and in-flight transactions settled once service resumed, but the incident is another data point for regulators weighing operational-resilience requirements for crypto exchanges that now double as card issuers and payment processors.

title: London Stock Exchange’s LSE 24 signals a bet on digital, AI-driven markets: TD Cowen
blurb: The London Stock Exchange will launch LSE 24, a 24/5 venue trading 17:00 to 07:50 GMT, with testing slated for end-2026 and exchange-traded products listing first in H1 2027. TD Cowen reads the move as a bet that digital, AI-driven order flow will keep trading active outside London hours, putting pressure on clearing and settlement operations built around a single daily cycle.

title: Bernstein Lifts Robinhood Target to $160 on Prediction Markets
blurb: Bernstein raised its Robinhood price target to $160 from $130, projecting that prediction-market revenue hits roughly $150M in Q2 2026 and overtakes crypto trading revenue for the first time. The call rests on Rothera, the CFTC-licensed exchange Robinhood built with Susquehanna out of LedgerX, which now handles about 16% of Robinhood's event-contract volume while it keeps distributing Kalshi's contracts.

title: Apple teams up with Klarna to launch a lease-to-own program for iPhones, iPads, and Macs
blurb: Apple is partnering with Klarna on Apple Upgrade, a lease-to-own program launching July 28 that spreads iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Watch purchases over 24- to 36-month terms, with some transactions carrying an added fee. The program replaces new sign-ups to iPhone Upgrade and arrives as memory-chip shortages push Apple hardware prices up, making financing a lever to protect unit volume.

title: OpenAI Names BNY, Nubank CEOs to Board Ahead of IPO
blurb: OpenAI added BNY CEO Robin Vince and Nubank CEO David Vélez to its board as it prepares for a public listing. The picks give OpenAI directors who have run a systemically important custody bank and a digital bank serving over 100M customers in Latin America, signaling the company wants regulatory and capital-markets fluency on the board before it faces public-market disclosure requirements.

title: Bank of Korea prepares for live CBDC transactions with 9 banks in September
blurb: The Bank of Korea will begin phase two of its CBDC pilot in September, expanding real-transaction testing to nine banks including KB Kookmin, Shinhan, Hana, and Woori. The BOK provides the infrastructure while each bank issues and manages its own deposit tokens, a split that mirrors the sponsor-bank model and runs in parallel with Korean banks building won-backed stablecoin rails.

title: Banks Wrap Up Loan Backing SoftBank’s $5.4 Billion Robotics Bet
blurb: A syndicate of banks closed the loan financing SoftBank's $5.4B robotics acquisition, upsizing the US dollar tranche after demand from institutional lenders exceeded the initial book. The upsizing signals leveraged-finance desks remain willing to underwrite SoftBank's balance sheet at scale, keeping debt markets open for the large tech and automation bets that have driven its recent dealmaking.

title: Treasury Flags Concern Over ‘Potentially Abusive’ Tax Trades
blurb: The US Treasury Department flagged concern over several high-profile tax strategies marketed by Wall Street banks, saying the structures may be "potentially abusive" and too good to be true. Treasury guidance or an IRS listing notice would force banks and wealth platforms to unwind or re-paper products already sold to clients, and any firm distributing the trades faces disclosure obligations and penalty exposure once a transaction is designated reportable.

title: Apple is reportedly teaming up with Klarna to let you pay off new devices over time
blurb: Apple is reportedly launching Apple Upgrade in the US on July 28, a Klarna-backed BNPL program offering 24-month installment plans for iPhone and Apple Watch and 36-month terms for iPad and Mac, per Bloomberg. Apple will stop accepting sign-ups for its bank-financed iPhone Upgrade Program once the new plan goes live, and AppleCare+ will no longer be bundled into the monthly payment.

title: UK Parliament begins inquiry into banking chokepoint for crypto businesses
blurb: The UK's Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group opened a cross-party inquiry into whether banks have systematically denied crypto firms accounts and imposed limits on crypto-related payments. The group is taking written evidence from banking, payments, fintech, and crypto firms over six weeks before publishing recommendations to the government, putting UK debanking practices on a formal policy track for the first time.

title: Seattle judge deals blow to Kalshi, rejects prediction market’s federal defense
blurb: King County Superior Court Judge John McHale granted Washington AG Nick Brown a preliminary injunction against Kalshi, rejecting the argument that CFTC oversight preempts state gambling law. The ruling makes Washington the latest state court to refuse Kalshi's federal-preemption defense, and both sides must submit proposed injunction language by Aug. 3 ahead of a full order Aug. 5.

title: Visa Launches Stablecoin Platform for 200M+ Merchants
blurb: Visa expanded its stablecoin settlement capabilities to merchant acquirers, launching pilots with Worldpay and Nuvei that settle USDC on Solana. Acquirers can now receive settlement in stablecoins rather than waiting on traditional fiat treasury movement between issuer and acquirer banks, which compresses cross-border settlement timing for merchants on a network that already reaches over 200M merchant locations.

title: BNPL Zip pulls out of New Zealand
blurb: Zip Co will shut down its New Zealand operations following a strategic review, redirecting capital to its Australian and US books, where quarterly income rose 20.2% to $335.2M and cash EBTDA hit a record $65.1M. The wind-down is immaterial to group financials, but it marks BNPL consolidating into scale markets as net bad debts crept up to 1.9% of transaction volume.
